summary: Case 4 (bootstrapped rep-measures) gates the group*interaction H0 TFCE on exist('ess2','var'), but case 4 never creates a variable named 'ess2' (it uses H0_ess2), so the interaction bootstrap TFCE is never generated.
detail: In case(4) the group*interaction branch builds H0_ess and H0_ess2 (lines 946/948/949/995) and saves H0_ess2 as the interaction H0 file (filename2, line 1009). The TFCE handling block at 1016-1023 tries to also run TFCE for the interaction with `if exist('ess2','var') && ~exist(...tfce_H0_ess_gp_interaction...)`. No variable 'ess2' exists anywhere in case(4) — that name is only created in case(3) at line 787. Therefore exist('ess2','var') is always false in case(4), the branch is unreachable, and the interaction-effect H0 TFCE file is silently never computed. Any subsequent TFCE-corrected inference on the group x repeated-measures interaction contrast has no null distribution and will fail or fall back incorrectly.
failure_scenario: User runs a 2nd-level group x repeated-measures contrast (multiple groups, so the else/group branch at line 942 executes and populates H0_ess2) with LIMO.design.tfce ~= 0, after having produced the observed interaction file ess_gp_interaction_<index>.mat via case 3. Case 4 saves the interaction bootstrap H0 (ess_gp_interaction_<index>_desc-H0.mat, line 1009) but, because exist('ess2','var') at line 1021 is always false in case 4, never calls limo_tfce_handling for the interaction. The interaction TFCE null file tfce_H0_ess_gp_interaction_<index>.mat is never created, leaving TFCE-corrected inference on the interaction contrast with no bootstrap null (subsequent correction fails or falls back incorrectly), while the main repeated-measures effect TFCE (line 1017-1019) is produced normally.
minimal_fix: At line 1021 replace exist('ess2','var') with exist('H0_ess2','var'). This correctly fires in the group branch (where H0_ess2 is created) and stays false in the single-group branch (lines 897-940, which never creates H0_ess2), matching the guard already used for saving the interaction H0 file at line 1003.
verifier_reasoning: Case 4 (bootstrapped rep-measures) runs lines 876-1026. The group*interaction bootstrap builds H0_ess2 (lines 946/949/995) and saves the interaction H0 file at line 1009 gated on exist('H0_ess2','var') (correct). But the TFCE guard at line 1021 tests exist('ess2','var'). The variable 'ess2' is assigned only inside case(3) (lines 787-845); MATLAB switch runs a single case, so 'ess2' is never in case(4)'s workspace. Hence line 1021 is always false and limo_tfce_handling for the interaction (tfce_H0_ess_gp_interaction_*) is never invoked. This is a copy/rename slip: the observed block at line 871 legitimately uses exist('ess2','var') because case 3 creates ess2, and the bootstrap block renamed ess->H0_ess / ess2->H0_ess2 everywhere except this guard. Result: bootstrap-under-H0 TFCE for the group x repeated-measures interaction contrast is silently never produced, so TFCE-corrected interaction inference has no null distribution. Not a duplicate of any listed finding (nearest is 769, a robust single-group horzcat crash with a different root cause). Severity high: definite loss of an entire correction result on a real supported path (2nd-level group x rep-measures contrast, gp_values>1, LIMO.design.tfce~=0), consistent with the file's other high correctness findings; niche path keeps it from critical.

summary: IRLS glm_iterate has the same defect: the full-model per-frame R is overwritten inside the interactions branch and then reused to compute the continuous-covariate F, corrupting covariate H0 statistics.
detail: In glm_iterate, R is the full-model residual-maker for the current frame (line 556, R = eye - WX*pinv(WX)). Inside the interactions branch it is reassigned at line 672 (reduced main-effects model) and again in the interaction loop at line 734 (R = eye - wx*pinv(wx)). WX (uppercase) is preserved. The continuous block at line 758 is an independent if that runs after the interactions branch, and at line 767 computes M = R0 - R with R0 from the full-model WX (line 765-766) but R stale from the last interaction sub-model. Thus F_continuous (line 770) and pval_continuous (line 771) for covariates in an IRLS N-way ANCOVA-with-interactions design are computed against an invalid projection, corrupting the null distribution.
failure_scenario: IRLS (method='IRLS') bootstrap-under-H0 with a factorial design that has 2+ categorical factors, at least one interaction term, and 1+ continuous covariate. glm_iterate runs the interactions branch (line 652), whose main-effects step (672) and interaction loop (734) overwrite R with sub-model residual-makers. The continuous block then computes M = R0 - R at line 767 using the full-model R0 (correct) but the stale interaction-sub-model R (wrong), so continuous.F and continuous.p returned for the H0 distribution are built from an invalid projection, yielding an incorrect covariate null distribution and therefore wrong bootstrap p-values / corrected thresholds for continuous covariate effects. No crash; silent statistical corruption.
minimal_fix: At the start of the continuous block (before line 762), restore the full-model residual-maker, e.g. insert `R = eye(size(Y,1)) - WX*pinv(WX);` (or reuse HM: `R = eye(size(Y,1)) - HM;`) so that M = R0 - R uses the full-model projection. WX/HM are preserved from lines 554-555, so recomputation is safe.
verifier_reasoning: Real defect in glm_iterate (limo_glm_boot.m). R is the full-model residual-maker set at line 556 (R = eye - WX*pinv(WX), WX uppercase = full weighted design). In the N-way-with-interactions branch (line 652), R is overwritten at line 672 (reduced main-effects sub-model) and again inside the interaction loop at line 734 (R = eye - wx*pinv(wx), interaction sub-model using lowercase wx). WX uppercase is never modified after line 554. The continuous block (line 758) is a separate `if nb_continuous ~= 0` that runs after the interactions branch. At line 765-766 it recomputes R0 fresh from the full-model WX, but at line 767 `M = R0 - R` reuses the stale interaction-sub-model R. Thus M mixes two different design projections (R0 from full X, R from an interaction sub-model x = [Main_effects ... I Cov_and_Mean]), so H (768), F_continuous (770) and pval_continuous (771) for covariates are computed against an invalid residual-maker. When continuous covariates are present the quick-path (line 704, gated on nb_continuous==0) is skipped and execution always enters the `else` interaction loop, so R is always corrupted before the continuous block. R0 itself is fine; only R is stale. Reachable with any IRLS design having 2+ factors + at least one interaction + at least one continuous covariate. Not a duplicate: the three known findings concern line 238 (1-way ANCOVA df scaling), line 748 (pval_interactions row-broadcast), and line 706 (quick-path df linear-indexing) — none involves the stale-R corruption of covariate H0 stats in the continuous block. Severity high: it silently corrupts the bootstrap null distribution for covariate effects (wrong F/p and hence wrong corrected thresholds) rather than crashing, but only for the specific factorial-ANCOVA-with-interactions IRLS design, so it is a genuine-but-scoped statistical correctness bug.

summary: Time-Frequency data (4D) is trial-shuffled with only three subscripts, mis-indexing/collapsing the trials dimension and later crashing.
detail: For Time-Frequency analyses Yr is a 4D cube [channels x freq x time x trials] — the code proves this itself: the placeholder at lines 48-49 allocates NaN(size(Yr,1),size(Yr,2),size(Yr,3),3,k) and line 69 indexes Data(channel,freq,:,testindex) with four subscripts. But line 59 does `Data = Yr(:,:,shuffling_index)` with only three subscripts. On a 4D array the third subscript spans the merged (time*trials) dimension, so shuffling_index (length = number of trials) picks a garbage subset and the result is a 3D array [channels x freq x N]. Design is shuffled correctly by trial at line 60, so Data and Design become misaligned, and line 55's array/find still assumes 4D. limo_prederror never calls limo_tf_4d_reshape, so nothing flattens Yr first. It should be `Yr(:,:,:,shuffling_index)` for the Time-Frequency case.
failure_scenario: Call limo_prederror(LIMO) (single argument, so N is defined) on a LIMO with LIMO.Analysis=='Time-Frequency' and a 4D Yr [channels x freq x time x trials]. Line 59 produces a 3D Data [channels x freq x trials] instead of the intended 4D trial-shuffled cube. At line 69, squeeze(Data(channel,freq,:,testindex)) indexes a nonexistent 4th dimension with testindex>1, throwing 'Index in position 4 exceeds array bounds (size 1)'. Were the crash avoided, Data would be trial-misindexed while Design (line 60) is trial-shuffled correctly, corrupting all prediction-error outputs.
minimal_fix: Branch on analysis type for the shuffle: for Time-Frequency use Data = Yr(:,:,:,shuffling_index); otherwise keep Data = Yr(:,:,shuffling_index);. (Alternatively reshape TF Yr to 3D up front as limo_AIC_BIC does.)
verifier_reasoning: Real, reachable bug. For Time-Frequency, Yr is 4D [channels x freq x time x trials] (confirmed by the 5D allocation at lines 48-49 and the 4-subscript indexing at lines 69-70). Line 58 sets shuffling_index = randperm(N) with N = size(Yr,4) = trials, but line 59 shuffles with only 3 subscripts: Yr(:,:,shuffling_index). On a 4D array the 3rd subscript addresses the merged (time*trials) dimension, so a trials-length index selects a garbage/partial subset and yields a 3D array [channels x freq x trials]. Line 69 then indexes a 4th dimension (Data(channel,freq,:,testindex)) of this 3D array with testindex spanning values >1, throwing 'Index in position 4 exceeds array bounds (size 1)'. Reached by the ordinary single-argument call limo_prederror(LIMO) where N is defined normally, so it is independent of the known nargin/N bug. The non-TF path (3D Yr) is unaffected because 3 subscripts fully address it. Distinct root cause and line from the already-known line-45 N-undefined finding, so not a duplicate. Also, even if it did not crash, the trials axis would be misindexed while Design (line 60) is shuffled correctly by trial, silently corrupting every TF prediction-error value.

summary: In getdata case 2, the Component single-channel branches reference LIMO.design.electrode instead of LIMO.design.component, so component analyses crash or return empty.
detail: getdata(2,...) serves two-samples t-test, N-Ways ANOVA and ANCOVA. For LIMO.Type='Components' with '1 channel/component only', the guard at line 2135 tests `length(LIMO.design.electrode)==1`, but match_channels never sets LIMO.design.electrode for Components (it sets LIMO.design.component, lines 1874/1901); electrode stays [] (init line 120). So the guard is always false and control drops to the 'multiple single channels' branch, where lines 2187/2189/2193/2195 index `tmp(LIMO.design.electrode(subject_nb),...)` = `[](subject_nb)`. The single-channel branch (lines 2153/2155/2159/2161) is also wrong, using `tmp(LIMO.design.electrode,...)`. Compare getdata case 1, which correctly uses LIMO.design.component (lines 2031/2065/2071).
failure_scenario: Run limo_random_select with a two-samples t-test (stattest==2; same applies to N-way ANOVA/ANCOVA), 'type','Components', analysis_type='1 channel/component only', and a single selected component so match_channels sets LIMO.design.component=5 while LIMO.design.electrode stays []. At line 2135 the guard length([])==1 is false, so execution enters the 'Use multiple single channels' branch. At line 2193 (non-TF con case) it evaluates tmp(LIMO.design.electrode(subject_nb),...) = tmp([](subject_nb),...), throwing 'Index exceeds the number of array elements (0)'. No group-level component result is produced. (If the guard were corrected to enter the single-channel branch, lines 2159/2161 would instead do tmp([],begins_at:ends_at,:), yielding an empty result and a dimension-mismatch assignment error.)
minimal_fix: In getdata case 2, replace LIMO.design.electrode with LIMO.design.component throughout the Components branches (single: 2153/2155/2159/2161 → LIMO.design.component; multi: 2187/2189/2193/2195 → LIMO.design.component(subject_nb)), and make the single/multi guard at line 2135 type-aware, e.g. `if (strcmpi(LIMO.Type,'Channels') && numel(LIMO.design.electrode)==1) || (strcmpi(LIMO.Type,'Components') && numel(LIMO.design.component)==1)`.
verifier_reasoning: Verified against source. In getdata case 2 (stattest==2: two-samples t-test, N-way ANOVA, ANCOVA), the '1 channel/component only' path guards on `length(LIMO.design.electrode)==1` (line 2135) and both the single- and multi-channel Components branches index `tmp` with `LIMO.design.electrode` (single: 2153/2155/2159/2161) or `LIMO.design.electrode(subject_nb)` (multi: 2187/2189/2193/2195). But for LIMO.Type='Components', match_channels only ever sets LIMO.design.component (lines 1874/1901); LIMO.design.electrode is initialized to [] (line 120) and never set. So (a) the guard `length([])==1` is always false, forcing the multi-channel branch even for a single selected component, and (b) that branch evaluates `tmp([](subject_nb),...)`, an index into an empty array. The parallel getdata case 1 correctly uses LIMO.design.component (lines 2031/2037/2065/2071), confirming this is a real electrode/component mix-up, not intended behavior. Even if the guard were fixed to enter the single branch, lines 2153-2161 still use the empty electrode, so both branches are broken for Components. Not a duplicate: no known finding references line 2135 or the case-2 Components electrode/component confusion (the closest, 1956, concerns case 1's backward loop). Severity high: a supported feature path (Component group stats with a single component) crashes deterministically with no result, though Component analyses are less common than Channel analyses.

summary: getdata case 1's single-vs-multi guard tests electrode length, so a scalar Component routes to the multi-channel branch and is indexed with the subject index, crashing for >1 subject.
detail: For one-sample t-test / regression with Type='Components' and '1 channel/component only', LIMO.design.electrode is [] (only LIMO.design.component is set by match_channels). The guard at line 2013 `if length(LIMO.design.electrode)==1` is therefore false even for a single component, so execution falls into the 'multiple single channels' else-branch (line 2046). There, lines 2065/2067/2071/2073 index `tmp(LIMO.design.component(i),...)` using the per-subject loop index i. A single scalar component works for i=1 but errors at i=2.
failure_scenario: One-sample t-test, 'type','Components', analysis_type='1 channel/component only', a single component entered so LIMO.design.component=3 (scalar) and LIMO.design.electrode=[], with N>=2 subjects. In getdata (stattest==1) the guard at line 2013 is false (length([])~=1), so execution enters the else branch (2046) and the Components sub-branch (2062). The subject loop at line 1956 runs backward (i=N:-1:1), so on the very first iteration i=N the code evaluates LIMO.design.component(N) on a scalar -> "Index exceeds the number of array elements" crash. (Report's "subject 2" is directionally right but with the backward loop the crash actually occurs on the first iteration for any N>=2.) The single-component group statistic is never computed.
minimal_fix: Make the guard type-aware, e.g. replace line 2013 with: `if (strcmpi(LIMO.Type,'Channels') && numel(LIMO.design.electrode)==1) || (strcmpi(LIMO.Type,'Components') && numel(LIMO.design.component)==1)` so a scalar component takes the single-channel path (lines 2028-2042), which indexes tmp(LIMO.design.component,...) constant across subjects. (Independent of the separate backward-loop/ordering fix tracked at line 1956.)
verifier_reasoning: Verified against source. For Type='Components' with '1 channel/component only', match_channels sets only LIMO.design.component (scalar, line 1901) and leaves LIMO.design.electrode=[] (init line 120, never assigned on the Components path). The single-channel guard at line 2013 tests `length(LIMO.design.electrode)==1`, which is `length([])==1` = false, so a single scalar component is misrouted into the else 'multiple single channels' branch (2046). Its Components sub-branch (lines 2065/2067/2071/2073) indexes `tmp(LIMO.design.component(i),...)` with the per-subject loop index i; for a scalar component, component(i) is out-of-bounds for any i>1. The Channels sibling case works only because the scalar there is stored in electrode (line 1897), which the guard checks — Components is uniquely broken. Real, deterministic crash for a legitimate, supported configuration; the single-component group stat is never computed. Not a duplicate: the known 1956 finding concerns the backward loop causing forward-index write ordering (silent data reversal), a separate root cause/symptom/fix; this is a type-blind guard causing a hard index-out-of-bounds crash at line 2013/2062-2073. Severity high: hard crash disabling a whole feature configuration, though limited to Components + single-component + N>=2.

summary: weighted_mean is read unconditionally but only assigned when Estimator1 is 'Mean'/'All', so choosing Trimmed mean / HD / Median crashes with an undefined-variable error.
detail: In the nargin==1 ERP branch, weighted_mean is set only inside `if strcmpi(Estimator1,'All') || strcmpi(Estimator1,'Mean')` at line 695. It is then read unconditionally at line 727 (`if strcmpi(weighted_mean,'yes')`), line 791, and line 854. limo_central_tendency_questdlg (verified) returns Estimator1 in {Mean, Trimmed mean, HD, Median}. When the user picks Trimmed mean, HD, or Median as the within-subject estimator, weighted_mean is never created and the first `strcmpi(weighted_mean,'yes')` at line 727 (or 787-791 for pooled) throws 'Unrecognized function or variable weighted_mean'.
failure_scenario: GUI call limo_central_tendency_and_ci('expected_chanlocs.mat') with a valid group-level dataset; in the estimator dialog pick 'Trimmed mean' (or 'HD' or 'Median') as Estimator1. Line 694's guard is false so weighted_mean is never defined. If Q=='Evaluate single conditions', line 727 `strcmpi(weighted_mean,'yes')` throws 'Unrecognized function or variable weighted_mean'; if Q=='Pool Conditions', the same error occurs at line 791; even a path skipping both still hits line 854. Execution aborts before any subject data is aggregated.
minimal_fix: Initialize `weighted_mean = 'no';` before the Estimator1 check (immediately after line 693), so the variable is always defined regardless of which estimator is chosen.
verifier_reasoning: In the nargin==1 GUI branch, `weighted_mean` is assigned only at line 695, inside `if strcmpi(Estimator1,'All') || strcmpi(Estimator1,'Mean')` (line 694). It is then read unconditionally at lines 727, 791, and 854. limo_central_tendency_questdlg returns Estimator1 in {Mean, Trimmed mean, HD, Median}. Choosing Trimmed mean / HD / Median leaves the line-694 guard false, so `weighted_mean` is never created. Execution then reaches `strcmpi(weighted_mean,'yes')` (line 727 for 'Evaluate single conditions', line 791 for 'Pool Conditions') and MATLAB throws 'Unrecognized function or variable weighted_mean'. This is a deterministic crash on the primary interactive entry point for 3 of the 4 available within-subject estimators, aborting before any data is processed. Not a duplicate: the known :360 (zero-padded trial dimension), :789 (non-scalar size() comparison), and :1069 (missing waitbar init) findings are distinct root causes; :789 is on an adjacent line but is an unrelated mechanism. Severity high: guaranteed crash blocking a whole class of common analyses, though it fails fast with no silent data corruption.

summary: Misspelled 'varagin' (missing r) on all argument-capture lines throws 'Undefined function or variable varagin'
detail: Lines 41, 42, 43, 45, 49, 52, and 53 read arguments via `varagin(...)` instead of `varargin(...)`. `varagin` is never defined, so the very first argument-capture line that executes raises 'Undefined function or variable "varagin"'. This is separate from the already-reported bug about `type` being captured as a cell via `varargin(1)`: here the identifier itself is misspelled, so no data/nboot/data1/data2 is ever assigned and the function cannot run at all.
failure_scenario: Call limo_compute_H0(0,data,nboot,'limo_trimci'). Line 35 sets type = varargin(1) (a 1x1 cell). Execution then crashes at line 38 `if type == 0` with "Operator '==' is not supported for operands of type 'cell'" — this is the known line-38 bug and it aborts before any `varagin` line runs. If line 38 is fixed to `type = varargin{1}` so type==0 is true, execution reaches line 45 `data = varagin(2)`, which then raises 'Undefined function or variable varagin'. Thus the misspelling is a real defect but is currently masked by the earlier line-38 crash; the reported claim that the abort occurs at line 45 is wrong.
minimal_fix: Rename every `varagin` to `varargin` on lines 41, 42, 45, 49, 52, 53, and switch argument capture to content indexing `varargin{n}` (not `varargin(n)`) so actual values rather than 1x1 cells are assigned; also fix line 35 to `type = varargin{1}` so line 38's comparison works.
verifier_reasoning:
The misspelled identifier `varagin` is genuinely present in the source at lines 41, 42, 45, 49, 52, 53 (the report also lists 43, but line 43 is correctly spelled `varargin(4)` — minor inaccuracy). `varagin` is never defined anywhere, so any line that executes it raises 'Undefined function or variable varagin'. This is a real, distinct defect from the known line-38 finding (which concerns `type` being captured as a cell via `varargin(1)`).

However, the reported failure SCENARIO is incorrect. Line 35 `type = varargin(1)` yields a 1x1 cell. Line 38 `if type == 0` then evaluates `{...} == 0`, which MATLAB rejects with "Operator '==' is not supported for operands of type 'cell'." That crash occurs BEFORE any `varagin` line is reached (the report claims the abort happens at line 45; it actually happens at line 38, which is the already-known bug). So as the code currently stands, the `varagin` lines are unreachable — they are masked by the earlier line-38 crash. Reachability requires first fixing line 38 to use `varargin{1}`, after which any call lands on line 45/49/52 and crashes on the misspelling.

Because the identifier is objectively undefined and would block the function the moment the earlier crash is fixed, this is a real latent defect (CONFIRMED), not the same root cause as the line-38 cell-indexing finding (not a duplicate). Severity high rather than critical: the function is already non-runnable due to line 38, so this defect's marginal impact is latent/masked, but it must be fixed for the function to ever execute.

summary: WLS multivariate weight loop reassigns the loaded 3D Betas array to a 2D limo_WLS output, corrupting it so the later Betas(:,t,:) assignment crashes.
detail: In case 4 multivariate, `load Betas` (line 434) loads the 3D coefficient array [channels x frames x params]. In the WLS branch the weight loop does `[Betas,W(e,:)] = limo_WLS(LIMO.design.X,squeeze(Yr(electrode,:,:))')`, overwriting Betas with limo_WLS's first output `b` (a 2D [params x frames] matrix). limo_WLS signature is `[b,W,rf] = limo_WLS(X,Y)`, so Betas is no longer the loaded 3D array. Then in the time loop, line 481 `Betas(:,t,:) = model.betas';` writes an [channels x params] slab into what is now a [params x frames] matrix, producing a size-mismatch assignment error. A scratch variable name (e.g. tmpB) should have been used instead of Betas.
failure_scenario: Run a Multivariate GLM (LIMO.design.type_of_analysis='Multivariate') with LIMO.design.method='WLS', LIMO.design.status='to do', and more than one non-NaN electrode. The weight loop at lines 451-453 overwrites the loaded 3D Betas with limo_WLS's 2D coefficient output [params x frames]. On the first time-frame iteration, line 481 `Betas(:,t,:) = model.betas';` attempts to write [electrodes x params] into a [params x 1] slice of the now-2D Betas, throwing a size-mismatch assignment error and aborting the analysis before any results are saved.
minimal_fix: Use a throwaway variable for the limo_WLS coefficient output at line 452: `electrode = array(e); [~,W(e,:)] = limo_WLS(LIMO.design.X,squeeze(Yr(electrode,:,:))');` leaving the loaded 3D Betas array intact for the time-frame loop at line 481.
verifier_reasoning: limo_WLS returns [b,W,rf] where b is a 2D [params x frames] matrix (limo_WLS.m:77). At limo_eeg.m:452 the multivariate WLS weight loop writes limo_WLS's first output into the loaded variable name `Betas` (`[Betas,W(e,:)] = limo_WLS(...)`), which was loaded at line 434 as the 3D coefficient array [channels x frames x params]. After the loop, `Betas` holds the last electrode's 2D [params x frames] matrix. In the time-frame loop, line 481 does `Betas(:,t,:) = model.betas';` where model.betas is [params x electrodes] (limo_mglm.m:36), so model.betas' is [electrodes x params]. On the (now) 2D `Betas`, the target `Betas(:,t,:)` resolves to column t = [params x 1], while the RHS has electrodes*params elements. With more than one electrode this is a left/right size-mismatch assignment error that aborts on the first time frame. Only when electrodes==1 would the counts coincide. The fix is to capture the WLS coefficients in a throwaway variable so the loaded 3D Betas survives to the time loop. This is not any of the listed known findings (all on other lines / root causes).

summary: WLS weight loop iterates 1:size(Yr,1) but indexes array(e) where array only holds non-NaN channels, causing an out-of-bounds crash when any channel is empty.
detail: `array = find(~isnan(Yr(:,1,1)))` (line 450) skips empty/NaN channels, so numel(array) can be less than size(Yr,1). The loop `for e = 1:size(Yr,1)` then evaluates `electrode = array(e)` (line 452), which indexes past the end of `array` once e exceeds the number of good channels. Additionally the weights are stored at `W(e,:)` (row e) rather than `W(electrode,:)`, so even before the crash the weights would be placed on the wrong channel rows. The loop bound should be `1:length(array)` and storage `W(electrode,:)`.
failure_scenario: Run a level-1 multivariate (limo_mglm) analysis with LIMO.design.method='WLS' on a data cube Yr [channels x time x subjects] in which at least one channel is all-NaN (a bad/rejected channel, exactly what the line-450 comment anticipates). array = find(~isnan(Yr(:,1,1))) then has fewer entries than size(Yr,1). The weight loop for e = 1:size(Yr,1) evaluates electrode = array(e) at line 452; once e exceeds numel(array), MATLAB throws 'Index exceeds the number of array elements', crashing the analysis before any results are produced. Even short of the crash, weights are written to W(e,:) rather than the true channel row W(electrode,:), so stored weights would be misaligned to the wrong channels.
minimal_fix: Change line 451 to `for e = 1:length(array)` and line 452 to store on the true channel row: `electrode = array(e); [Betas,W(electrode,:)] = limo_WLS(LIMO.design.X,squeeze(Yr(electrode,:,:))');` — mirroring the correct idiom in limo_glm_handling.m.
verifier_reasoning: Verified against the codebase's own canonical implementation of the identical operation. limo_glm_handling.m (lines 97-98, 201-229, 465-466) uses the correct idiom: array = find(~isnan(Yr(:,1,1))); for e = 1:length(array); channel = array(e); ... W(channel,...). The multivariate branch in limo_eeg.m (case 4, lines 450-452) instead loops for e = 1:size(Yr,1) and stores W(e,:). The comment on line 450 ('skip empty electrodes') proves the author intends array to be shorter than size(Yr,1) when NaN/bad channels are present. When that happens, numel(array) < size(Yr,1) and array(e) at line 452 throws 'Index exceeds the number of array elements' once e exceeds the number of good channels — a hard crash. Secondarily, W(e,:) misplaces weight rows relative to the channel-indexed read pattern used everywhere else, though the crash from the loop bound masks this. When all channels are good, e==electrode and the code is harmless, which is why the bug survived. Not a duplicate: none of the eight known limo_eeg.m findings (158, 405, 502, 516, 577, 722, 792, 833) touch this loop/root cause. Severity high: a definite crash in a realistic, author-anticipated scenario (multivariate WLS with any bad/all-NaN channel), aborting the analysis.

summary: WLS/IRLS per-channel df cells are indexed by absolute channel number instead of loop position, so any skipped/flat channel leaves an empty cell that makes the cell2mat consolidation crash.
detail: In the non-OLS branch, df info is stored as LIMO.model.model_df{channel} (and conditions_df/interactions_df/continuous_df) at lines 141-149, keyed by 'channel' = array(e), the ABSOLUTE channel index. At level 1, array = find(~isnan(Yr(:,1,1))) (line 41), which is non-contiguous whenever leading channels are NaN, and additionally any channel whose data are flat produces model=[] (lines 118-120) so its cell is never assigned. On the final iteration (e==size(array,1)) the code runs cell2mat(LIMO.model.model_df)' at line 154 (and again at 164, 175, 186). cell2mat over a cell array containing empty [] entries errors ('Dimensions of arrays being concatenated are not consistent'), aborting the whole first-level WLS/IRLS analysis at the very end after all channels were computed. Even when it does not error, the size checks at lines 155/165/176/187 compare against size(Yr,1)*2 (all channels) while the cell count equals length(array), so the df reshaping is silently misaligned when array != 1:nchan. The OLS branch is unaffected (it does not use cells). Correct code would key the cells by e (loop index) and size against numel(array).
failure_scenario: First-level WLS (or IRLS) analysis, LIMO.Type='Channels'. Case 1 (leading bad channel): channel 1 is NaN so array=[2;3;...;64]. All 63 good channels compute; cells are stored at model_df{2..64} leaving model_df{1}=[]. On the last iteration cell2mat drops the leading empty, yielding a 1x126 (63-pair) vector; the size check vs size(Yr,1)*2=128 fails, so df/dfe end up as 63 rows offset by one channel relative to the 64-row R2/Betas/Yhat, and for the single-factor conditions branch the size(Yr,1) checks miss entirely and conditions_df is filled with stale model_df values — silently wrong dfe saved in LIMO.mat, corrupting downstream p/effect-size. Case 2 (last channel flat): array=1:64 but channel 64 is all-zero -> model=[]; the consolidation block never runs, so LIMO.model.model_df/conditions_df remain cell arrays in the saved LIMO.mat, causing downstream code that does numeric arithmetic on them to error. Both cases require the non-OLS path; OLS is unaffected.
minimal_fix: Key the temporary df cells by the loop counter e instead of the absolute channel: LIMO.model.model_df{e}=model.df; and likewise conditions_df{e}/interactions_df{e}/continuous_df{e} at lines 141/143/146/149. Change the consolidation size comparisons from size(Yr,1) to numel(array) at lines 155/165/167/176/178/187/189 so they match the compact per-analyzed-channel count. Additionally, move the consolidation (the e==size(array,1) block) out from under the ~isempty(model) guard (or trigger it on the last non-empty channel) so a flat final channel cannot leave model_df as an unconsolidated cell.
verifier_reasoning: Real defect confirmed in the non-OLS (WLS/IRLS) branch of limo_glm_handling.m. Cells are keyed by the ABSOLUTE channel index (channel=array(e), lines 141/143/146/149), while at level 1 array=find(~isnan(Yr(:,1,1))) (line 41) is non-contiguous whenever leading channels are NaN, and any flat channel yields model=[] (line 119) so its cell is never assigned. The final-iteration consolidation cell2mat(LIMO.model.model_df) (lines 154/164/175/186) plus the size checks against size(Yr,1)*2 / size(Yr,1) (lines 155/165/167/176/178/187/189) therefore misbehave when array != 1:nchan. The OLS branch (lines 125-136, scalar df, no cells) is unaffected, so normal contiguous full-channel OLS is fine — matching the report. One mechanism correction: cell2mat on the auto-created ROW cell reduces to cat(2,...), which silently DROPS 0x0 empty entries rather than throwing 'Dimensions... not consistent'. So the leading-NaN / interior-flat cases do NOT crash at cell2mat; they produce a short, channel-misaligned df matrix (numel(assigned) rows vs the size(Yr,1) rows used by R2/Betas/Yhat), and because the size checks compare to size(Yr,1) the reshape then either reuses stale df/dfe from the previous block or writes df into the wrong channel rows — silent statistical corruption of the saved dfe used downstream for p-values/effect sizes. A genuine hard failure the report under-weighted: if the LAST processed channel is flat (model=[]), the entire consolidation block (guarded by ~isempty(model), line 123) is skipped, so LIMO.model.model_df is saved into LIMO.mat as an unconsolidated cell array, breaking downstream numeric consumers. Reachable with realistic inputs (NaN'd bad channels or all-zero/flat channels are common in level-1 EEG). Not a duplicate: the three known findings are at lines 717 (H0 covariate filename), 820 (TFCE interaction loop bound), and 449 (H0 conditions preallocation) — different root causes/lines; line 154 df-cell keying is not among them.

summary: WLS-TF single-covariate regression never assigns model.continuous.p, so the post-loop reshape crashes (and the in-loop df assignment is also malformed).
detail: In the WLS-TF case, the simple-regression branch (nb_factors==0 && nb_continuous==1) at lines 821-823 assigns only model.continuous.F and model.continuous.df inside the frequency loop; it never assigns model.continuous.p. After the loop, line 889 `isfield(model,'continuous')` is true (because .F exists), the code enters the `nb_factors==0 && nb_continuous==1` branch, and line 892 executes `model.continuous.p = reshape(model.continuous.p, [n_freqs*n_times,1])`, referencing a field that was never created -> 'Unrecognized field name p'. Contrast with the OLS/WLS simple-regression branch (lines 509-511) which correctly sets .F, .df AND .p. Separately, line 823 `model.continuous.df(:,freq) = [1 (size(Y,2)-rank(X))]` assigns a 1x2 row into a single-column slice of a not-yet-existing field, which is itself a shape-mismatched assignment; either way this code path is broken.
failure_scenario: Run limo_glm on a Time-Frequency dataset with method WLS (-> WLS-TF), a design of one continuous covariate and no categorical factors. Execution reaches line 892 (or 823) and errors instead of returning model.continuous.
minimal_fix: Inside the simple-regression branch add `model.continuous.p(:,freq) = p_Rsquare;` and fix the df assignment orientation (e.g. `model.continuous.df(freq,:) = [1 (size(Y,2)-rank(X))];`), mirroring the OLS/WLS branch.

After the loop, line 889 isfield(model,'continuous') is true because .F exists, so line 890's condition (nb_factors==0 && nb_continuous==1) is taken and line 892 executes model.continuous.p = reshape(model.continuous.p, [n_freqs*n_times,1]) — referencing a field that was never created, producing 'Unrecognized field name p'. summary: WLS-TF N-way (no-interaction) main effects store F_conditions transposed, so model.conditions.F ends up dimensioned [n_times x n_freqs x nb_factors] and the post-loop reshape loop indexes the wrong dimension and errors.
detail: For nb_factors>1 without interactions, line 679 does `model.conditions.F(:,freq,:) = F_conditions';`. F_conditions is nb_factors x n_times (line 648), so F_conditions' is n_times x nb_factors and the resulting array model.conditions.F is [n_times, n_freqs, nb_factors] (factor is the 3rd dim, not the 1st). The post-loop reshape at lines 864-866 iterates `for f = length(nb_conditions):-1:1` and calls `reshape(model.conditions.F(f,:,:), [n_freqs*n_times,1])`, treating the FIRST dimension as the factor index. model.conditions.F(f,:,:) has numel n_freqs*nb_factors, but the target is n_freqs*n_times, so reshape errors unless nb_factors==n_times. The df assignment at line 680 `model.conditions.df(:,freq,:) = [df_conditions ; repmat(dfe,...)]` is likewise shape-inconsistent with the preallocation [nb_factors x n_freqs x 2] (RHS is 2 x nb_factors), scrambling df/dfe or erroring. The same defect is duplicated in the interactions branch main-effects code at lines 741-742.
failure_scenario: Run limo_glm on a Time-Frequency dataset with method 'WLS' (auto-promoted to 'WLS-TF' at line 99) and a purely categorical N-way design with nb_factors>=2 and no interactions (e.g. a 2x2 factorial, nb_conditions=[2 2], nb_interactions empty, nb_continuous=0). Typical sizes n_freqs~20, n_times~100. The per-freq loop stores F_conditions transposed at line 679, making model.conditions.F [n_times, n_freqs, nb_factors]. Execution then reaches the reshape loop at line 864; model.conditions.F(f,:,:) has n_freqs*nb_factors elements while reshape targets n_freqs*n_times, so MATLAB errors 'reshape: number of elements must not change' (nb_factors=2 != n_times=100). Even without the crash, the transposed layout plus first-dim indexing would silently return wrong per-factor F/p maps.
minimal_fix: At line 679 remove the transpose so the factor stays on dim 1: `model.conditions.F(:,freq,:) = F_conditions;` (yielding [nb_factors, n_freqs, n_times], which the reshape loop's F(f,:,:) indexing expects). Align the df write at line 680 to the preallocated [nb_factors, n_freqs, 2] shape: `model.conditions.df(:,freq,:) = [df_conditions' repmat(dfe,numel(df_conditions),1)];`. Apply the same two corrections to the interactions-branch main-effects code at lines 741-742.
verifier_reasoning: Real crash, verified against the code. In WLS-TF, Y is reshaped to [n_freqs, N_subjects, n_times] (lines 181-190), so size(Y,3)=n_times and F_conditions (line 648) is [nb_factors x n_times]. At line 679 `model.conditions.F(:,freq,:) = F_conditions'` writes a [n_times x nb_factors] slice into the un-preallocated model.conditions.F, so the array becomes [n_times, n_freqs, nb_factors] (factor on dim 3). The post-loop reshape (lines 864-866) does `for f = length(nb_conditions):-1:1: reshape(model.conditions.F(f,:,:), [n_freqs*n_times,1])`, treating dim 1 as the factor index; F(f,:,:) has numel n_freqs*nb_factors, but the target requires n_freqs*n_times, so reshape throws a size-mismatch error whenever nb_factors != n_times (always, in practice). The identical bug exists in the interactions-branch main-effects code at lines 741-742. The df write at line 680 is also shape-scrambled ([2 x nb_factors] RHS into preallocated [nb_factors, n_freqs, 2] slice) but the F reshape crashes first. Not a duplicate: all known findings cover other locations — the interactions reshape block (876/883), E4 (750), IRLS df/p (1110/1152), OLS/WLS SE (285), HC4 SE (599). None address the conditions main-effect store at 679/741 or the conditions reshape at 864-866. Severity high: hard crash on a mainstream design (Time-Frequency + WLS = default weighting, any >=2-factor categorical ANOVA without interactions).

summary: Discriminant projection multiplies eigenvectors by Y in the wrong order/orientation, crashing the 1-factor MANCOVA path
detail: In the 1-way block, when covariates are present Eigen_vectors_cond is assigned (line 214) so the discriminant branch (else at 269) actually executes. `a = inv(chol(E))*Eigen_vectors_cond` is p-by-p (p = number of electrodes = size(Y,2)). The projection loop does `z(:,d) = a(:,d)'*Y`, i.e. (1-by-p) * (n-by-p). The inner dimensions (p and n) do not agree, so MATLAB throws 'Incorrect dimensions for matrix multiplication'. To obtain discriminant scores per observation the code must compute `Y*a(:,d)` (n-by-1). This is independent of the already-reported guard bug (267) and the no-covariate undefined-variable bug (270): those kill the *no-covariate* discriminant, whereas this crash bites the *covariate* (MANCOVA) case which otherwise reaches line 275.
failure_scenario: Call limo_mglm with a 1-way design having one categorical factor plus one continuous covariate (nb_conditions>0, nb_continuous>0), with n observations and p electrodes where n≠p, and n large enough that the guard at 267 passes (length(Y)-nb_conditions > nb_conditions). Execution enters the covariate branch (line 206), assigns Eigen_vectors_cond (214), computes a=inv(chol(E))*Eigen_vectors_cond (270, p-by-p), then at 275 evaluates a(:,d)'*Y = (1×p)*(n×p) → 'Incorrect dimensions for matrix multiplication', aborting the function.
minimal_fix: Replace line 275 `z(:,d) = a(:,d)'*Y;` with `z(:,d) = Y*a(:,d);` to yield n-by-1 discriminant scores.
verifier_reasoning: At line 275, `a` is p-by-p (E=Y'*R*Y is p-by-p with p=size(Y,2), line 183; Eigen_vectors_cond from limo_decomp(E,H) is p-by-p; inv(chol(E)) is p-by-p). So `a(:,d)'` is 1-by-p and Y is n-by-p; `a(:,d)'*Y` is (1×p)*(n×p), an inner-dimension mismatch that errors whenever n≠p (the generic case). This reaches runtime specifically in the 1-way MANCOVA path (nb_conditions≠0 && nb_continuous≠0), where Eigen_vectors_cond is assigned at line 214 so line 270 succeeds and control flows to 275. Not a duplicate: known finding 270 is the no-covariate path crashing earlier on the unassigned Eigen_vectors_cond and never reaching 275; known finding 267 is the length(Y) guard. This is a separate root cause — wrong operand orientation of the discriminant projection. (Note: z is computed but unused/dead since the classification block is empty, but the error still aborts limo_mglm, so it is a genuine crash on a valid input.)

summary: N-Ways ANOVA menu option is padded with spaces, so the string passed to limo_random_select fails its exact-match validation and errors out.
detail: In ANOVA_Callback the questdlg option for the N-way model is the literal padded string '     N-Ways ANOVA     ' (line 319). Whatever the user picks becomes `answer`, and `answer` is passed verbatim as the stattest argument to limo_random_select at lines 338/341. limo_random_select validates it at line 88-92 with tests = {...,'N-Ways ANOVA',...} and `if sum(strcmpi(stattest,tests))==0, error('input argument error, stat test unknown'), end`. strcmpi is an exact (case-insensitive but not trimmed) comparison, and '     N-Ways ANOVA     ' != 'N-Ways ANOVA' because of the leading/trailing spaces, so the sum is 0 and limo_random_select throws. The 'ANCOVA' and 'Repeated Measures ANOVA' options are unpadded and match, so only the N-way path is broken. Note the internal update_dir dispatch at line 323 uses the same padded literal so it works there, masking the problem until limo_random_select is reached.
failure_scenario: User opens the random-effect GUI with handles.type = 'Channels', clicks the ANOVA button, and selects the default-visible N-way model. answer becomes '     N-Ways ANOVA     '. update_dir succeeds (its comparison at line 323 uses the same padded literal). test_chan_loc passes, then line 338 calls limo_random_select('     N-Ways ANOVA     ', handles.chan_file, ...). limo_random_select's validation (line 90) finds no match against 'N-Ways ANOVA' and errors 'input argument error, stat test unknown'. N-way ANOVA cannot be launched from the GUI at all.
minimal_fix: Trim the label before passing it downstream, e.g. add `answer = strtrim(answer);` right after the limo_questdlg call (before the strcmpi dispatch, adjusting the line-323 comparison to 'N-Ways ANOVA'), or pass the canonical 'N-Ways ANOVA' string explicitly in the limo_random_select calls at lines 338/341 for the N-way case.
verifier_reasoning: Verified against source. Line 318-319: limo_questdlg's N-way option is the padded literal '     N-Ways ANOVA     '. limo_questdlg returns the chosen button label verbatim into `answer`. Line 323's internal dispatch uses the same padded literal so update_dir succeeds (masking the fault). But lines 338/341 pass `answer` (still padded) as the stattest argument to limo_random_select. limo_random_select line 88-92 validates with `sum(strcmpi(stattest,tests))==0 -> error`, where tests contains the trimmed 'N-Ways ANOVA'. strcmpi is case-insensitive but NOT whitespace-trimming, so '     N-Ways ANOVA     ' fails to match and the function throws 'input argument error, stat test unknown'. The ANCOVA and Repeated Measures ANOVA labels are unpadded, so only the N-way path is broken. Root cause (padded menu label vs exact-match validation) is distinct from the known line-148 num2bouble typo, so not a duplicate. Severity high: complete, deterministic loss of GUI N-way ANOVA functionality for any user reaching line 338/341; it is a hard crash rather than silent corruption and is GUI-only (limo_random_select can still be called directly with the correct string), so not critical.

summary: Bootstrap H0 save for Repeated-Measures ANOVA uses squeeze() which drops the singleton channel dimension for single-channel analyses, causing a size-mismatch assignment error.
detail: At line 1402 `H0_Rep_ANOVA(:,:,:,:) = squeeze(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA(:,:,i,:,:));` (and identically at line 1413 for `H0_Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_with_gp`). `tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA` is [channels,frames,nb_effects,2,bootstrap]. When there is only 1 channel, `squeeze(...(:,:,i,:,:))` collapses BOTH the singleton channel dim (dim 1) and the singleton effect dim (dim 3), yielding a [frames,2,bootstrap] 3-D array, while the LHS `H0_Rep_ANOVA` was preallocated 4-D as NaN(1,frames,2,bootstrap). The explicit `(:,:,:,:)` assignment then fails with a left/right size mismatch. The authors already knew about this: the corresponding observed-effect save at lines 1169-1170 deliberately uses `reshape` 'instead of squeeze in case there is only 1 channel', but the bootstrap/H0 saves here still use squeeze.
failure_scenario: Run a single-channel repeated-measures ANOVA (size(data,1)==1, e.g. one ICA component or a single channel of interest), any type 1-4, with LIMO.design.bootstrap>0. The parfor bootstrap (lines 1294-1395) completes successfully, then the save loop is entered. At line 1401 H0_Rep_ANOVA is preallocated as NaN(1,frames,2,bootstrap) (4-D). Line 1402 evaluates squeeze(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA(:,:,i,:,:)) = squeeze of [1,frames,1,2,bootstrap], which collapses to the 3-D [frames,2,bootstrap]. The assignment H0_Rep_ANOVA(:,:,:,:) = <[frames,2,bootstrap]> then errors: 'Unable to perform assignment because the size of the left side is 1-by-frames-by-2-by-bootstrap and the size of the right side is frames-by-2-by-bootstrap.' The identical crash occurs at line 1413 for type 3/4 interaction-with-group. All H0 files and subsequent TFCE/correction are lost.
minimal_fix: At line 1402 replace squeeze with reshape mirroring lines 1170/1195: H0_Rep_ANOVA = reshape(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA(:,:,i,:,:), [size(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA,1) size(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA,2) size(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA,4) size(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA,5)]); (dropping the preallocation on 1401, or keeping it and assigning into (:,:,:,:) with the reshaped 4-D result). Apply the identical reshape at line 1413 for H0_Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_with_gp using size(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_with_gp, ...).
verifier_reasoning: Real bug. tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA is [channels,frames,nb_effects,2,bootstrap]. In the H0 save loop, line 1401 preallocates H0_Rep_ANOVA = NaN(size(...,1),size(...,2),size(...,4),size(...,5)); for a single channel this is 4-D size [1,F,2,K]. Line 1402 assigns squeeze(tmp_boot_H0_Rep_ANOVA(:,:,i,:,:)). For channels==1 the slice is [1,F,1,2,K]; squeeze removes BOTH singleton dims (channel dim 1 and effect dim 3), producing a 3-D [F,2,K]. The subscripted assignment H0_Rep_ANOVA(:,:,:,:) = [F,2,K] requires shape match (numel matches but the leading singleton on the LHS shifts dimensions, so [1,F,2,K] vs padded [F,2,K,1] differ), and MATLAB throws the classic 'size of the left side is 1-by-... right side is ...' error — same failure the authors deliberately avoided at line 1170 with reshape (their comment even says 'use reshape instead of squeeze in case there is only 1 channel'). For multi-channel the shapes align so it works, confirming the bug is single-channel-specific. Line 1413 has the identical defect for H0_Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_with_gp (type 3/4). Not a duplicate: the known 1342 finding concerns XB=X(...) group-design linear indexing, a different root cause; no known finding covers the squeeze-vs-reshape single-channel save. Severity high: it crashes after the full parfor bootstrap has run, losing all H0 and downstream TFCE/FDR/cluster correction output, though only in single-channel repeated-measures ANOVA runs with bootstrap>0.

summary: t-test cluster correction never squares T because the guard tests lowercase 'ttest' while t-test filenames use capital 'Ttest'
detail: In the MCC==2 cluster branch the code does `if contains(FileName,'ttest') || contains(FileName,'LI_Map')` then calls `limo_clustering(M.^2,Pval,bootM.^2,bootP,...)`, else calls it on the raw signed values. LIMO saves t-test files as 'One_Sample_Ttest_parameter_N', 'Paired_Samples_Ttest_...', 'Two_Samples_Ttest_...' (capital 'Ttest', confirmed in limo_random_robust.m lines 171/337/506). MATLAB `contains` is case-sensitive, so `contains(FileName,'ttest')` is always FALSE for real t-test files. The dispatch that actually sets M at line 180 correctly uses capital 'Ttest', so M (signed T values) is populated and reaches line 328, but the squaring branch is dead. Cluster correction therefore runs on signed T / signed bootT instead of T^2, so negative-going effects fall below threshold and the cluster-forming statistic is wrong.
failure_scenario: Display a One_Sample_Ttest_parameter_1 result (or Two_Samples/Paired_Samples) with MCC=2 (spatial-temporal clustering) where the true effect is negative-going. Dispatch at line 180 sets M to signed T values. At line 290 the MCC==2 branch is entered (M non-empty, H0 file present), reaching line 328. contains(FileName,'ttest') is false because the filename contains capital 'Ttest', so line 331 runs, passing signed T and signed bootT to limo_clustering. There the observed negative cluster's mass is a negative sum, while the H0 max-cluster-mass distribution is built from signed-T sums (predominantly positive), so the corrected cluster p-value approaches 1 and the mask is empty — a genuinely significant negative t-cluster is silently reported as non-significant, producing statistically wrong corrected results.
minimal_fix: At line 328 make the match case-insensitive to align with the capital 'Ttest' dispatch guard: change `if contains(FileName,'ttest') || contains(FileName,'LI_Map')` to `if contains(FileName,'Ttest','IgnoreCase',true) || contains(FileName,'LI_Map')` (equivalently change 'ttest' to 'Ttest'). This restores the intended T^2 squaring at line 329. (Separately, the lowercase strfind(FileName,'ttest') at lines 185/265 corrupts the title string and should also be corrected, but that is out of scope for this defect.)
verifier_reasoning: Verified against real code. Dispatch at limo_stat_values.m:180/260 matches t-test files with capital contains(FileName,'Ttest') and populates M with signed T values (matfile(:,:,:,4), not squared). Confirmed t-test result filenames use capital 'Ttest' (limo_random_robust.m:171/337/506: One_Sample_Ttest_parameter_%g, Two_Samples_Ttest_..., Paired_Samples_Ttest_...). The MCC==2 squaring guard at line 328 uses lowercase contains(FileName,'ttest'); MATLAB contains is case-sensitive, so it is always FALSE for real t-test files and the T^2 branch (line 329, the file's only .^2) is dead. Execution instead hits line 331 passing signed T/bootT into limo_clustering. In limo_clustering.m the cluster mass is sum(bootM_b(posclusterslabelmat==C)) then max(tmp(:)); with signed T a negative-going real cluster yields a negative mass compared against a predominantly-positive H0 max-cluster-mass distribution, so its corrected p approx 1 and it is wrongly declared non-significant. Squaring to T^2 (F-equivalent; limo_clustering documents its M input as F values, matching every other F-valued input) is what enables the correct two-sided cluster test. The MCC==2 branch (line 290) is reachable with M non-empty and an existing H0 file. Not a duplicate: known findings are at lines 201 (R2 dispatch/extension stripping), 135 (TF H0 infix), 456/503/510 (single-channel TF Rep_ANOVA bootstrap dim) — different lines and root causes. Note LI_Map is unaffected (same case in both guards); only t-tests break.

summary: Single-channel time-frequency con/t-test H0 TFCE indexes a pre-squeezed 3D array with a spurious 4th subscript, crashing for every bootstrap after the first.
detail: In the con/One-sample/Two-samples/Paired branch, line 217 does H0_tval = squeeze(H0_tval(:,:,:,end-1,:)). For a single-channel analysis H0_tval starts as [1 x freq x time x 5 x nboot]; selecting end-1 gives [1 x freq x time x 1 x nboot] and squeeze removes BOTH the leading channel singleton and the stat singleton, yielding a 3-D array [freq x time x nboot]. The parfor body at line 219 then calls limo_tfce(2,H0_tval(:,:,:,b),[],0). Indexing a 3-D array with four subscripts requires the 4th (b) to reference a singleton trailing dimension: b=1 returns the ENTIRE [freq x time x nboot] cube (all bootstraps, not slice b), and b>=2 throws 'Index in position 4 exceeds array bounds'. Even the b=1 case then mismatches the assignment tfce_H0_score(1,:,:,1) which expects [1 x freq x time]. The correct slice is H0_tval(:,:,b). Contrast with the R2 branch (line 153) and F branch (line 289), which squeeze inside the loop and index the correct dimension. The observed-data path for single channels works; only this H0 path is broken.
failure_scenario: Run a single-channel (one electrode/component) one-sample, two-samples, or paired t-test, or apply TFCE to a con_ file, on Time-Frequency data with bootstrap+TFCE enabled. Execution enters the branch at lines 214-219. tfce_H0_score is allocated as [1 x freq x time x nboot] (correct), H0_tval is squeezed to 3-D [freq x time x nboot] at line 217, and the parfor at line 219 calls limo_tfce(2, H0_tval(:,:,:,b), [], 0). The b>=2 iterations throw 'Index in position 4 exceeds array bounds' (and b=1 silently returns the whole cube), so the H0 TFCE null distribution never computes and TFCE-corrected inference for the single-channel TF t-test/contrast fails.
minimal_fix: At line 219 change H0_tval(:,:,:,b) to H0_tval(:,:,b), since H0_tval is 3-D [freq x time x nboot] after the line-217 squeeze; this matches how tfce_H0_score(1,:,:,b) selects the b-th bootstrap and mirrors the correct pattern in the R2/F branches.
verifier_reasoning: At line 217 the code pre-squeezes H0_tval: original single-channel TF t/con array is [1 x freq x time x 5 x nboot]; H0_tval(:,:,:,end-1,:) is [1 x freq x time x 1 x nboot] and squeeze removes BOTH the channel and stat singletons, producing a 3-D [freq x time x nboot]. Line 219 then indexes this 3-D array with four subscripts H0_tval(:,:,:,b). For b=1 the 4th subscript hits an implicit trailing singleton and returns the whole [freq x time x nboot] cube (all bootstraps, not slice 1), which is passed to limo_tfce type 2 and assigned into tfce_H0_score(1,:,:,1) that expects [1 x freq x time] -> mismatch/garbage. For b>=2 (always, nboot is hundreds) MATLAB throws 'Index in position 4 exceeds array bounds', aborting the parfor and the entire H0 TFCE null. The observed-data path (lines 192-205) squeezes and indexes 3-D correctly, so only the null distribution is broken. The R2 branch (153) and F branch (289/303) squeeze inside the loop with the correct (:,:,:,2,b) index, confirming line 219 is uniquely wrong. Correct index is H0_tval(:,:,b). Distinct from known findings: not a wrong-tfce-type issue (line 219 uses the correct type 2) and not the R2 thmaps-capture issue; different line, branch, and root cause.

summary: Single-channel non-TF con/t-test H0 TFCE both mis-sizes tfce_H0_score and indexes the wrong bootstrap dimension after squeezing away the channel singleton.
detail: In the same con/t-test branch, non-time-frequency single-channel case: line 223 does H0_tval = squeeze(H0_tval(:,:,end-1,:)). H0_tval starts [1 x time x 5 x nboot]; after selecting end-1 and squeezing, both the channel singleton and stat singleton vanish, giving a 2-D array [time x nboot]. Two defects follow: (1) line 224 preallocates tfce_H0_score = NaN(1,size(H0_tval,2),nboot) but size(H0_tval,2) is now nboot (not time), so the array is NaN(1,nboot,nboot) with the wrong middle dimension. (2) line 226 calls limo_tfce(1,H0_tval(:,:,b),neighbouring_matrix,0): H0_tval(:,:,b) on a 2-D array is the whole matrix for b=1 and out-of-bounds for b>=2. The correct slice is H0_tval(:,b). Note the TF sibling (line 216) preallocates BEFORE the squeeze so its size is right, but this non-TF path preallocates AFTER the squeeze (line 224), compounding the error.
failure_scenario: Run a single-channel (one electrode / single-source ROI) one-sample, two-samples, or paired t-test, or a con analysis, on ERP or Power (non-Time-Frequency) data with TFCE bootstrap enabled, so that an H0 file exists and size(H0_tval,1)==1. Control reaches lines 222-227. Line 223 squeezes H0_tval to 2-D [time x nboot]. Line 224 allocates tfce_H0_score as NaN(1,nboot,nboot) (middle dim wrongly = nboot instead of time). The parfor at b=2 executes H0_tval(:,:,2) on a 2-D array, throwing an index-out-of-bounds error; even b=1 passes the entire [time x nboot] matrix into limo_tfce type 1 (which expects a 1-D vector) against a mis-sized destination. The null TFCE distribution is never built and thresholding/correction fails.
minimal_fix: Preallocate before the squeeze and index the bootstrap dimension correctly. Replace lines 222-227 with: neighbouring_matrix = LIMO.data.neighbouring_matrix; tfce_H0_score = NaN(1,size(H0_tval,2),nboot); % size(,2)=time before squeeze; H0_tval = squeeze(H0_tval(:,:,end-1,:)); parfor b=1:nboot, [tfce_H0_score(1,:,b),tfce_H0_thmaps{b}] = limo_tfce(1,H0_tval(:,b),neighbouring_matrix,0); end. (Equivalently, mirror the F-branch: keep H0_tval unsqueezed and use squeeze(H0_tval(:,:,end-1,b)) inside the loop.)
verifier_reasoning: Real bug confirmed by reading limo_tfce_handling.m:221-227. For a single-channel (size(H0_tval,1)==1) non-Time-Frequency con/one-sample/two-samples/paired analysis, H0_tval starts [1 x time x 5 x nboot]. Line 223 does H0_tval=squeeze(H0_tval(:,:,end-1,:)): selecting end-1 leaves [1 x time x 1 x nboot], and squeeze collapses the channel singleton (dim1) AND the stat singleton (dim3), producing a 2-D [time x nboot] array. Two consequences: (1) line 224 preallocates NaN(1,size(H0_tval,2),nboot), but post-squeeze size(H0_tval,2)==nboot, so the array is NaN(1,nboot,nboot) with the wrong middle dimension; (2) line 226 calls limo_tfce(1,H0_tval(:,:,b),...): H0_tval(:,:,b) indexes a nonexistent 3rd dim of a 2-D array — whole matrix for b=1, out-of-bounds error for b>=2 (always hit since nboot is hundreds/thousands). The correct slice is H0_tval(:,b). The TF sibling (215-217) preallocates before the squeeze and is safe; the F-branch single-channel non-TF path (293-295) never globally squeezes and squeezes per-slice, so it is correct — this con/t branch uniquely squeezes-then-preallocates-then-mis-indexes. Not a duplicate: the three known findings (303 multi-chan TF type 2 vs 3; 289 single-chan TF type 1 vs 2; 153 R2 thmaps assignment) are different lines and root causes.

summary: spm_bwlabel is called with a logical argument (no double() cast) on its preferred code path, which crashes because spm_bwlabel requires a double input.
detail: Lines 56 (4D branch) and 87 (3D branch) call spm_bwlabel(squeeze(bootp(...))<=alphav, 6). The first argument is a logical array. The spm_bwlabel MEX gateway requires a double-precision input and errors on non-double data. Every sibling function casts explicitly: limo_ecluster_make.m:58, limo_ecluster_test.m:61, and limo_tfcluster_test.m:60/92 all use spm_bwlabel(double(...),6). limo_tfcluster_make is the only one that omits the cast, and spm_bwlabel is the FIRST-preferred branch (exist('spm_bwlabel','file')==3 is checked before bwlabeln), so on any system that has SPM installed the H0 threshold computation aborts immediately.
failure_scenario: A user with SPM's compiled spm_bwlabel MEX on the MATLAB path (exist('spm_bwlabel','file')==3) runs a time*frequency second-level analysis with cluster correction, invoking limo_tfcluster_make to build the null cluster-mass distribution. On the first bootstrap iteration, spm_bwlabel receives the logical mask squeeze(bootp(...))<=alphav and throws an error (input must be a double array), so no TF cluster threshold is produced and the analysis aborts. Affects both the 4D electrode*freq*time*boot branch (line 56) and the 3D freq*time*boot branch (line 87).
minimal_fix: Wrap the logical masks in double() to match the sibling functions: line 56 -> spm_bwlabel(double(squeeze(bootp(E,:,:,kk))<=alphav),6); line 87 -> spm_bwlabel(double(squeeze(bootp(:,:,kk))<=alphav),6).
verifier_reasoning: Verified against real code. limo_tfcluster_make.m:56 (4D branch) and :87 (3D branch) call spm_bwlabel(squeeze(bootp(...))<=alphav, 6). The relational `<=alphav` produces a MATLAB logical array. The spm_bwlabel MEX gateway (spm_bwlabel.c) requires prhs[0] to be a real double array and calls mexErrMsgTxt on non-double input, so a logical mask throws immediately. The spm_bwlabel branch is guarded by `exist('spm_bwlabel','file')==3` and is checked BEFORE the bwlabeln fallback, so on any installation with SPM's compiled MEX on the path this is the taken path and it aborts. Cross-checked all four sibling cluster functions (limo_ecluster_make.m:58, limo_ecluster_test.m:61, limo_tfcluster_test.m:60, :92) — every one explicitly wraps the comparison in double(); limo_tfcluster_make is the sole omission, strong corroboration that the cast is required and its absence is a defect. Not statistical subtlety — a hard crash preventing the H0 TF cluster-mass threshold from ever being computed. Severity high (not critical) because it requires SPM's spm_bwlabel MEX to be present; systems relying on the Image Processing Toolbox bwlabeln fallback (which accepts logical) are unaffected, so it is not universal. No already-known findings listed, so not a duplicate.

summary: The default-alpha guard tests nargin<3 but alphav is the 4th argument, so calling with the documented default (3 args) leaves alphav undefined and the function crashes.
detail: Signature is limo_tfcluster_test(orif,orip,th,alphav). The guard at line 44 reads `if nargin < 3; alphav = 0.05; end`, but to default the 4th argument it must test `nargin < 4`. When the function is called with 3 arguments (orif,orip,th) — the documented way to accept the default alpha of 0.05 — nargin==3, `3 < 3` is false, so alphav is never assigned. The very next use at line 54 (U = round((1-alphav)*b)) then references an undefined variable and errors. There is no other assignment to alphav anywhere in the function.
failure_scenario: Call limo_tfcluster_test(orif,orip,th) with any valid 3D F/p matrices and a th struct containing a 'max' field (the documented way to request the default alpha=0.05). nargin==3, so the `nargin < 3` guard does not fire and alphav stays undefined. Execution reaches line 54, U = round((1-alphav)*b), which errors with 'Unrecognized function or variable alphav' before any cluster thresholding occurs.
minimal_fix: Change line 44 from `if nargin < 3` to `if nargin < 4`.
verifier_reasoning: Signature is limo_tfcluster_test(orif,orip,th,alphav); alphav is the 4th argument. The guard at line 44 tests `nargin < 3` instead of `nargin < 4`. The docs (FORMAT line 11, INPUTS line 16) state alpha defaults to 0.05, i.e. the intended default-alpha call passes exactly 3 args (orif,orip,th). With 3 args nargin==3, `3 < 3` is false, so alphav is never assigned — and lines 44-46 are the only assignment to alphav in the function. It is then dereferenced at line 54 (round((1-alphav)*b)) and lines 58/60/68 (orip<=alphav), producing an 'Unrecognized function or variable alphav' error before any clustering runs. Confirmed by reading the full function; no other assignment exists. No in-toolbox caller invokes it (grep finds only a 'See also' doc mention), so it is not hit by internal pipelines, but the documented public API for default alpha is entirely broken — hence high, not critical. Distinct from the known line-69 finding (per-cluster full-map overwrite), so not a duplicate.

summary: In the Time-Frequency component-clustering branch, `nb_subjects` is set to the count of UNIQUE subjects, but it is used to `repmat` for a cellfun against the per-dataset list, causing a size mismatch (crash) when subjects have more than one dataset.
detail: Line 265 sets `nb_subjects = length(unique({STUDY.datasetinfo.subject}))`. Line 269 then does `find(cellfun(@strcmp, dsetinfo', repmat({data_dir},nb_subjects,1)))`. `dsetinfo` is built from `{STUDY.datasetinfo.filepath}` so its length equals the number of datasetinfo entries (D). cellfun requires both cell arrays to be the same size, so `repmat({data_dir},nb_subjects,1)` must produce D elements. The Frequency sibling branch (line 162) deliberately uses `length({STUDY.datasetinfo.subject})` (= D, non-unique) — with the `unique` form explicitly commented out — precisely so the sizes match. The TF branch uses the `unique` form, so whenever the number of unique subjects is less than the number of datasets, `nb_subjects != D` and cellfun throws 'All of the input arguments must be of the same size and shape'.
failure_scenario: Run a Time-Frequency Components analysis with LIMO.data.cluster ~= 0 and an EEGLAB STUDY in the base workspace where at least one subject contributes more than one dataset (so unique subjects U < datasetinfo entries D). At line 267 dsetinfo becomes a 1xD row cell (rel2fullpath always grows a row), so line 269's dsetinfo' is D x 1 while repmat({data_dir},nb_subjects,1) is U x 1. cellfun(@strcmp, D x 1, U x 1) errors: "All of the input arguments must be of the same size and shape." (Both operands are columns; the reporter's 1xD-vs-Dx1 description mislabels the shapes but the size mismatch and crash are correct.)
minimal_fix: Change line 265 to nb_subjects = length({STUDY.datasetinfo.subject}); (drop unique), matching the Frequency branch at line 162 so the repmat count equals the D datasetinfo entries used by dsetinfo.
verifier_reasoning: At line 265 the TF component-clustering branch sets nb_subjects = length(unique({STUDY.datasetinfo.subject})) (U unique subjects), while the parallel Frequency branch (line 162) deliberately uses length({STUDY.datasetinfo.subject}) (D datasetinfo entries) with the unique form commented out. dsetinfo is produced by rel2fullpath, which builds file_fullpath{i} for i=nit:-1:1, always yielding a 1xD row cell; thus at line 269 dsetinfo' is D x 1 and repmat({data_dir},nb_subjects,1) is U x 1. cellfun(@strcmp, dsetinfo', repmat(...)) requires both cell inputs to have identical size/shape, which holds only when U == D. Whenever any subject contributes more than one dataset (U < D), cellfun throws "All of the input arguments must be of the same size and shape." The branch is reachable via a Time-Frequency Components analysis with LIMO.data.cluster ~= 0 and an EEGLAB STUDY loaded, a normal configuration when subjects have multiple conditions/sessions saved as separate datasets. Not a duplicate: known findings on this file are line 271 (newY dimensionality), 150 (icawinv component count), 289 (unconditional limo_struct2mat), and 255 (double squaring); none concern the nb_subjects/unique size mismatch. Severity medium: hard crash but confined to the TF component-clustering path with multi-dataset subjects, matching the reporter's assessment.

summary: Contrast estimability/invariance test is vacuous: it projects a vector already guaranteed to lie in col(X), so the invalid-contrast branch (go=0) is unreachable.
detail: In the 2-argument validity path the code computes lambda = X*C' (line 118), then check = int16(X*pinv(X) * lambda) and compares it to int16(lambda) (lines 117-120). But lambda = X*C' is by construction a linear combination of the columns of X, i.e. it already lies in the column space of X. The projector P = X*pinv(X) maps any vector already in col(X) to itself, so P*lambda == lambda holds identically (up to floating-point noise far below the int16 rounding grid). Consequently sum(check) always equals size(X,1), the `go = 0` branch on line 122 is never taken, and every contrast is reported valid. A correct estimability test must check that the contrast lies in the ROW space of X, e.g. C*(pinv(X)*X) approx == C. LIMO routinely uses rank-deficient design matrices (full dummy coding plus an intercept), so non-estimable contrasts genuinely exist and are silently accepted.
failure_scenario: On a rank-deficient 2nd-level design (full dummy coding + intercept, e.g. X columns [g1 g2 g3 intercept] with g1+g2+g3=intercept), call go = limo_contrast_checking(C, X) with a non-estimable contrast such as C=[1 1 -1 0]. This fails the ones-sum test (line 112), fails the weighted-sum test (line 114), passes the constant guard (line 131, C(end)=0), and enters branch 3. Because lambda=X*C' lies in col(X) by construction, P*lambda==lambda and go=1 is returned even though C is not in the row space of X (C(4)=0 != C(1)+C(2)+C(3)=1). The intended invalid-contrast guard never fires, so limo_batch_contrast / limo_contrast proceed to compute a meaningless, non-estimable contrast instead of erroring.
minimal_fix: Replace the column-space test with a row-space (estimability) test on C. Compute H = pinv(X)*X (projector onto the row space of X) and require the whole contrast row to be fixed by it: check = ( int16(C*H) == int16(C) ); if sum(check) ~= length(C), go = 0; else go = 1; end. This correctly flags contrasts C not in the row space of X as invalid on rank-deficient designs, while leaving estimable contrasts valid.
verifier_reasoning: The estimability test at lines 117-122 is mathematically vacuous. lambda = X*C' (line 118) is by construction a linear combination of X's columns, hence lies in col(X). P = X*pinv(X) (line 117) is the orthogonal projector onto col(X), which fixes every vector already in col(X). So P*lambda == lambda holds identically; residual numerical error is O(eps*norm), orders of magnitude below the int16 rounding grid (0.5). Therefore check==int16(lambda) is all-true, sum(check)==size(X,1) always, and the go=0 branch (line 122) is unreachable — every contrast reaching branch 3 is reported valid. I confirmed branch 3 is genuinely reachable and admits real non-estimable contrasts: with a rank-deficient full-dummy-plus-intercept design [g1 g2 g3 intercept] (g1+g2+g3=intercept, p=4), C=[1 1 -1 0] passes the sum-of-ones test (sum=1 != nnz=3), fails the weighted-sum test, passes the constant-term guard (C(end)=0), reaches branch 3, and returns go=1 — yet it is not estimable because row-space membership requires C(4)=C(1)+C(2)+C(3)=1, not 0. LIMO routinely builds rank-deficient categorical designs, so non-estimable contrasts genuinely exist and are silently accepted. The correct test is row-space membership of C (C*pinv(X)*X approx == C), not column-space membership of X*C'. Not a duplicate: the known findings are line 92 (bogus nested LIMO.LIMO.contrast field on save) and line 124 (go overwritten per loop iteration for multi-row F contrasts); this is a distinct defect in the projection logic itself. Severity medium, not higher: it is a broken validation guard that fails to reject bad user-supplied contrasts rather than corrupting a valid workflow — valid contrasts still return go=1, and the constant-term guard (line 131) plus the two earlier branches catch common cases, so real-world blast radius requires a user to actively pass a non-estimable contrast on a rank-deficient design.

summary: Case 4 single-group branch saves H0_ess with `save([subname filename], ...)` where filename is already an absolute path (built at line 881), corrupting the save path when subname is non-empty; the else branch correctly uses `save(filename, ...)`.
detail: Line 881 sets filename = fullfile(LIMO.dir,['H0' filesep 'ess_' num2str(index) '_desc-H0.mat']) — a full absolute path. In the gp_values==1 branch, line 940 does `save([subname filename], 'H0_ess', '-v7.3')`, prepending the subname string (e.g. 'sub-01_desc-') in front of the absolute path. The group branch at line 1001 correctly does `save(filename, ...)` without subname. With a non-empty subname the concatenation yields a relative path like 'sub-01_desc-/Users/.../ess_1_desc-H0.mat' interpreted relative to cwd (LIMO.dir/H0), whose parent directory does not exist, so save errors out or writes to the wrong location. Even with empty subname the two branches are inconsistent.
failure_scenario: Run a second-level repeated-measures bootstrapped contrast (type 4 → case 4) with a single group (LIMO.design.nb_conditions == 1) where LIMO.dir's path contains a BIDS 'sub-XX' token so limo_get_subname returns non-empty and subname becomes 'sub-XX_desc-'. Line 883 changes cwd to LIMO.dir/H0. After the parfor bootstrap finishes, line 940 executes save('sub-XX_desc-/Users/.../H0/ess_1_desc-H0.mat', 'H0_ess', '-v7.3'); this is a relative path into a nonexistent directory, so save throws and the H0_ess results are lost. The functionally parallel else branch (line 1001) uses save(filename,...) and succeeds, proving the intended behavior. With an empty subname line 940 happens to work, but is still inconsistent with lines 853/1001.
minimal_fix: Change line 940 from `save([subname filename], 'H0_ess', '-v7.3');` to `save(filename, 'H0_ess', '-v7.3');` to match line 1001 (filename is already the absolute H0 path; subname is not part of H0 naming in this 2nd-level branch).
verifier_reasoning: Real, verified inconsistency. In case 4 (2nd-level bootstrapped repeated-measures contrast), `filename` is built at line 881 as a full absolute path via fullfile(LIMO.dir, ['H0' filesep 'ess_' num2str(index) '_desc-H0.mat']) and contains no subname. The gp_values==1 branch at line 940 saves with `save([subname filename], ...)`, prepending subname to that absolute path, whereas the group else branch of the SAME case at line 1001 correctly does `save(filename, ...)` with no subname. subname (line 210-213) is non-empty whenever LIMO.dir's path contains a BIDS 'sub-' token (limo_get_subname), and the code deliberately uses it elsewhere (case 3, lines 853/862) where filename is *relative* — so the maintainers do expect it to be non-empty in this function. When non-empty, [subname filename] = 'sub-XX_desc-/Users/.../H0/ess_1_desc-H0.mat', a relative path (no leading '/') that MATLAB resolves against the cwd set at line 883 (LIMO.dir/H0), pointing into a nonexistent intermediate directory; save errors and the completed parfor H0_ess bootstrap is lost. If subname is empty (typical group dir) [subname filename]==filename and it works, so no silent corruption, but the two branches remain inconsistent and line 1001 establishes the correct intent (H0 filename does not carry subname since filename is already absolute). Not a duplicate: no known finding covers line 940 or the subname/absolute-path concatenation (510 concerns the _desc-H0 suffix vs the stat-values reader, a different root cause). Severity medium: hard crash losing expensive bootstrap output, but only in the narrow case of a subject-scoped LIMO.dir path.

summary: The multivariate bootstrap (case 2, Multivariate) transposes and matrix-multiplies the full N-D Betas array (`Betas'*X'*M*X*Betas`) instead of a per-time 2-D slice, which errors on N-D transpose; the surrounding block also linear-indexes Y/centered_data with boot_table(:,B).
detail: In case(2) the Multivariate branch (lines 675-730) never slices Betas by time or bootstrap. Betas here is the loaded (H0) parameter array, at least 3-D (channels x time x beta, and 4-D channels x time x beta x nboot for H0). Line 717 computes H = (Betas'*X'*M*X*Betas). MATLAB's ' (ctranspose) is undefined for N-D arrays, so this throws 'Transpose on ND array is not defined.' Additionally line 686/687/690 do Y = centered_data(boot_table(:,B)) and X = design(boot_table(:,B)), which linear-index the multidimensional arrays into vectors rather than resampling rows, so even if the transpose were fixed the statistics would be meaningless. The multivariate contrast bootstrap is non-functional.
failure_scenario: User calls limo_contrast(Yr, 'H0/..._Betas_desc-H0.mat', LIMO, 'T', 2, C) where LIMO.design.type_of_analysis=='Multivariate' and LIMO.design.bootstrap is set. Execution reaches the case(2) Multivariate block (line 675); on the first bootstrap (e=1,B=1) line 717 evaluates Betas' on the 4-D H0 Betas array and MATLAB throws "Transpose on ND array is not defined", aborting the contrast. (With a scalar contrast the crash is at 717; with a longer C it may instead abort at the uncaught eye(rank(X))-c*pinv(c) dimension mismatch on line 712 — a crash regardless.)
minimal_fix: Mirror the case(1)/mass-univariate slicing: wrap the statistic in a per-time-frame loop and index Betas as squeeze(Betas(:,time,:,B)) (2-D channels x betas) before the matmul H = B2'*X'*M*X*B2. Separately, resample the subject dimension properly instead of linear-indexing: Y = centered_data(:,:,boot_table(:,B)); X = design(boot_table(:,B),:); (and use squeeze(Y(:,time,:)) per frame), rather than centered_data(boot_table(:,B)) / design(boot_table(:,B)). Also accumulate/save results per frame rather than overwriting `result` in the loop.
verifier_reasoning: In case(2) (bootstrap) the Multivariate branch (lines 675-730) is reached whenever LIMO.design.type_of_analysis=='Multivariate' and type==2, since Method (line 178) is that field and this block is a standalone `if` distinct from the Mass-univariate block. In type==2, Betas is loaded as the H0 bootstraps array (lines 70-81), which is 4-D (channels x frames x betas x nboot). Line 717 computes `Betas'*X'*M*X*Betas` using ctranspose directly on that N-D array. MATLAB's ' is undefined for arrays with ndims>2, so `Betas'` throws "Transpose on ND array is not defined." — a hard crash on B=1. This contrasts with the correct sibling code that slices per frame/boot: line 479 uses squeeze(Betas(:,time,:)) and line 589 uses squeeze(Betas(channel,:,:,B)). The report's second point is also correct: lines 686/687/690 linear-index centered_data/design/Y with boot_table(:,B), collapsing the [chan x frame x subj] cubes into vectors rather than resampling the subject dimension, so even without the crash the statistics are meaningless (and the loop at 726 overwrites `result` every iteration and never saves, so the whole branch is non-functional WIP). The crash is guaranteed for a scalar (T) contrast; for a multi-element C the try/catch at 708-713 may instead throw at 712 on a dimension mismatch, but a crash occurs either way. None of the known findings (231, 298, 416/417, 510, 553, 571, 595, 630, 631, 769, 162, 165) reference line 717 or the case(2) multivariate block, so this is not a duplicate. Severity medium: definite crash but confined to the rarely-exercised multivariate-bootstrap-contrast path, consistent with the report.

summary: F_CONTVALUES/p_CONTVALUES have inconsistent orientation between OLS/WLS and IRLS: OLS/WLS transpose to [frames x ncovariates] (line 451-452) while IRLS returns [ncovariates x frames] untransposed (line 540, 770).
