detail: In the OLS/WLS branch, F_CONTVALUES{B} = F_continuous' and p_CONTVALUES{B} = pval_continuous' (lines 451-452), where F_continuous is NaN(nb_continuous,size(Y,2)); the transpose makes the stored cell [frames x nb_continuous]. In the IRLS path, glm_iterate allocates F_continuous = NaN(nb_continuous,size(Y,2)) (line 540) and returns it WITHOUT transposing, so F_CONTVALUES{B} is [nb_continuous x frames]. The two methods therefore hand back covariate F/p cubes with swapped axes. For nb_continuous>1 (multi-covariate regression/ANCOVA) any downstream code in limo_glm_handling that assumes a single orientation will index the wrong axis for one of the two method families; for nb_continuous==1 the row/column mismatch can also break concatenation.
failure_scenario: Run a Time-Frequency, first- or second-level analysis with method=IRLS, a design with nb_continuous>=2 covariates, and bootstrap H0 enabled. Per freq f, glm_iterate returns model{f}.continuous.F{B} as [nb_continuous x n_times]. At limo_glm_handling.m:576, tmp_H0_Covariates(channel,f,:,i,1,B) = model{f}.continuous.F{B}(:,i) selects column i = a [nb_continuous x 1] vector (frame i across covariates), not the intended [1 x n_times] covariate-i time course; assigning a [nb_continuous x 1] source into the [1 x n_times] destination throws a dimension-mismatch error and aborts the bootstrap whenever nb_continuous != n_times (nearly always). The equivalent OLS/WLS run stores [n_times x nb_continuous] so (:,i) is correct there, confirming the two method families hand back covariate H0 cubes with swapped axes. (Note: the ERP/spec consumer at lines 619-626 is unaffected because it auto-detects orientation.)
minimal_fix: Make IRLS match the OLS/WLS convention: in glm_iterate, before returning, transpose the continuous outputs when non-empty, e.g. after line 774 add: if nb_continuous ~= 0, F_continuous = F_continuous'; pval_continuous = pval_continuous'; end. This yields [frames x nb_continuous] identical to the OLS/WLS branch, which both the adaptive ERP consumer (619-626) and the unconditional TF consumer (575-578) then handle correctly. Alternatively drop the transpose in the OLS/WLS branch (lines 451-452) and switch the TF consumer to (i,:), but transposing in glm_iterate is the smaller, lower-risk change.
verifier_reasoning: The orientation inconsistency is genuine: OLS/WLS store F_CONTVALUES{B}/p_CONTVALUES{B} transposed to [frames x nb_continuous] (limo_glm_boot.m:451-452), while the IRLS helper glm_iterate fills F_continuous(n,frame)/pval_continuous(n,frame) and returns them untransposed as [nb_continuous x frames] (line 775 transposes only Betas). The reporter's claim that ANY downstream code breaks is partly overstated: the ERP/spec bootstrap consumer (limo_glm_handling.m:619-626) has an adaptive size check that selects (:,i) vs (i,:) and therefore handles BOTH orientations correctly, so that path is fine. However the Time-Frequency bootstrap consumer (limo_glm_handling.m:575-578) indexes model{f}.continuous.F{B}(:,i) unconditionally with no orientation check, and the IRLS TF boot path is reachable (limo_glm_handling.m:480-491, per-freq limo_glm_boot call at 488). For IRLS multi-covariate the untransposed [nb_continuous x n_times] cube makes (:,i) return a [nb_continuous x 1] column (frame i across covariates) rather than the intended [1 x n_times] covariate-i vector, which is both the wrong data and the wrong shape for the [1 x n_times] destination slot. nb_continuous==1 is safe (vector, element count matches). Not a duplicate of the three known findings (:238 df scaling, :748 p-row broadcast, :706 interaction df linear-index) — distinct root cause. Severity medium: real but requires the specific combination Time-Frequency + IRLS + nb_continuous>=2 + bootstrap; outcome is a hard crash (dimension mismatch, aborting H0) or, in the improbable nb_continuous==n_times case, silently corrupted/transposed covariate H0 maps.

summary: getdata case 2 iterates groups descending and subjects ascending while match_frames indexes the flattened first_frame/last_frame with groups ascending and subjects descending, so per-subject trim offsets are applied to the wrong subjects.
detail: match_frames flattens a cell-of-cells Paths as `for gp=1:size` (ascending) and inner `for s=size:-1:1` (descending), building first_frame/last_frame in that order (lines 1640-1655). getdata case 2 reads subjects with `for igp=length:-1:1` (descending groups) and `for i=1:size` (ascending subjects), incrementing subject_nb 1,2,3... Thus subject_nb=1 is group-last/subject-1 but first_frame(1) belongs to group-first/subject-last. begins_at/ends_at (lines 2101/2110) are then computed from the wrong subject's trim indices.
failure_scenario: Two-samples t-test (stattest==2), 2 groups. Subjects were run at first level with different epoch windows so limo.data.trim1/trim2 differ across subjects (e.g. g1 subjects trimmed to [-100,500] ms, g2 subjects to [-200,600] ms), giving non-constant first_frame/last_frame. match_frames stores these in flattened order (groups ascending, subjects descending). getdata case 2 loads subjects in the opposite order (groups descending, subjects ascending) but indexes first_frame(subject_nb)/last_frame(subject_nb) by the incrementing subject_nb, so begins_at=max(first_frame)-first_frame(subject_nb)+1 and ends_at=size(tmp,2)-(last_frame(subject_nb)-min(last_frame)) are computed from a DIFFERENT subject's trims than the one in tmp. The global max/min are still correct, but each subject's data is cropped with another subject's offset, so the time/freq frames no longer correspond across subjects. The resulting group data cube is misaligned frame-by-frame, producing wrong (silently corrupted) t-values with no error. When subjects additionally differ in channel count, subj_chanlocs(subject_nb) points to the wrong subject, so the length==size(tmp,1) check can spuriously fail (subject dropped) or pass and hand limo_match_elec the wrong chanlocs.
minimal_fix: Index first_frame/last_frame/subj_chanlocs in getdata case 2 using the same flattened order match_frames produces (groups ascending, subjects descending), rather than by a monotonically increasing subject_nb over the (groups descending, subjects ascending) traversal. Concrete options: (a) iterate getdata case 2 as `for igp=1:length(LIMO.data.data)` with inner `for i=size(LIMO.data.data{igp},2):-1:1` and increment subject_nb inside, so subject_nb matches the match_frames flatten index (keeping the per-group output buffer keyed on igp so group assignment stays correct); or (b) precompute an explicit map from (igp,i) to the match_frames flattened position and use map(igp,i) wherever first_frame/last_frame/subj_chanlocs are indexed in place of subject_nb. Either aligns the trim/chanloc metadata with the subject actually loaded.
verifier_reasoning: match_frames flattens the cell-of-cells Paths as groups ascending / subjects descending (lines 1643-1648) and fills first_frame/last_frame/subj_chanlocs in that flattened index order (loop i=size:-1:1, lines 1655,1692-1706,1683). getdata case 2 (stattest==2) instead walks groups DESCENDING (igp=length:-1:1, line 2089) and subjects ASCENDING (i=1:size, line 2091), incrementing subject_nb 1,2,3,... It then reads first_frame(subject_nb)/last_frame(subject_nb)/subj_chanlocs(subject_nb) (lines 2101-2111, 2114-2115, etc.) to compute begins_at/ends_at for the subject actually loaded at (igp,i). Concrete trace (2 groups, 2 subjects each): first_frame index order = [g1s2,g1s1,g2s2,g2s1]; getdata subject_nb order = [g2s1,g2s2,g1s1,g1s2]. Every subject_nb thus pulls a different subject's trim offsets (subject_nb=1 loads g2s1 but reads first_frame(1)=g1s2). LIMO.data.data{igp}{i} and Paths{gp}{sub} share the same within-group subject index (built at lines 489-491), so the physical files line up — only the metadata index is scrambled. When first_frame/last_frame are constant across subjects (all share trim1/trim2) begins_at=1/ends_at=size, so the mismatch is harmless; but when subjects have heterogeneous epoch windows the per-subject begins_at:ends_at is taken from the wrong subject, silently misaligning channel-by-frame data across the group and corrupting the two-samples statistics. subj_chanlocs(subject_nb) is likewise mis-indexed, so the length(...)==size(tmp,1) guard can wrongly discard a subject or feed limo_match_elec another subject's chanlocs when channel counts differ. Not a duplicate: the known critical at 1956 is getdata case 1, where first_frame(i) is correctly aligned to the loaded subject and only the OUTPUT write index is reversed; this is case 2 at line 2089 with a distinct root cause (metadata lookup order vs match_frames flatten order). Severity medium: silent statistical corruption, but only triggered when subjects carry different trim windows and/or channel structures — the common homogeneous-trim two-samples test is unaffected.

symbol: Repeated Measures ANOVA (con files, paths provided)
summary: The con-file 'paths provided' branch is unreachable because `all(size(LIMO.data.data(i,j)))` is always true, and it would use char subscripts `{num2str(i),num2str(j)}` if reached.
detail: At line 1356 `if all(size(LIMO.data.data(i,j)))` uses () indexing which always yields a 1x1 cell, so size==[1 1] and all()==true for every i,j; the elseif at line 1359 is dead code. The intended distinction (single path string vs. a provided list) is lost, so when the user passes an already-expanded list of file paths the code still calls `limo_get_files([],[],[],LIMO.data.data{i,j})` (line 1357) as if it were a path-to-a-list file. If the elseif were ever reached, `LIMO.data.data{num2str(i),num2str(j)}` indexes the cell with char codes (e.g. '1'->49) -> out-of-range error, and `LIMO.data.data{i}{i,j}` (line 1360) is also wrong nesting.
failure_scenario: Command-line Repeated-Measures ANOVA with con files where the caller supplies LIMO.data.data{i,j} as a cell array of already-resolved per-subject con file paths (the same convention the beta branch supports via breaklimofiles). Because the line 1356 condition is always true, control enters line 1357 and passes the cell to limo_get_files's path2file argument; cell2mat concatenation errors on unequal-length paths (or, when lengths match, exist() fails on the garbage-concatenated string), so the files are never loaded and the analysis aborts at line 1363. The pre-resolved-path-list case is unreachable through the intended elseif at 1359, which is also itself broken (char subscripts via num2str and wrong nesting).
minimal_fix: Mirror the working beta branch by testing the cell contents, not the 1x1 wrapper, and indexing with {i,j}: replace lines 1356-1361 with `if ischar(LIMO.data.data{i,j}) % path to a list file` calling `limo_get_files([],[],[],LIMO.data.data{i,j})`, and `else % list of paths provided` calling `breaklimofiles(LIMO.data.data{i,j})`. This removes the always-true size() test, the char num2str subscripts, and the wrong {i}{i,j} nesting.
verifier_reasoning: Line 1356 `if all(size(LIMO.data.data(i,j)))` uses parenthesis indexing on a cell array, yielding a 1x1 cell whose size is always [1 1], so all()==true for every i,j; the elseif at 1359 is provably dead. This mirrors the beta-file branch (1277-1281) which correctly tests `ischar(LIMO.data.data{i})` to distinguish a single path-to-a-listfile from a pre-resolved cell of paths and routes the latter to breaklimofiles. The con-file branch loses that distinction: a caller passing LIMO.data.data{i,j} as a cell array of already-resolved con paths is wrongly sent to line 1357, which hands that cell to limo_get_files as path2file. In limo_get_files (lines 58-60) cell2mat on differing-length path strings throws a dimension error; with equal lengths it produces a concatenated garbage string that fails exist(...,'file') at line 97, hits the errordlg/return, yielding empty Names and aborting at 1363. The dead elseif, if ever reached, is additionally broken: num2str(i)/num2str(j) char subscripts index by char code (out-of-range) and LIMO.data.data{i}{i,j} is wrong nesting. Not a duplicate: no known finding covers lines 1354-1361 or the con-file provided-list routing.

symbol: Repeated Measures ANOVA (Full scalp, Components, Time-Frequency)
summary: For Components + Time-Frequency in the repeated-measures full-scalp branch, trimming uses `tmp(:,begins_at:ends_at,:)` where begins_at/ends_at are 2-element vectors, so only the first element is used and the result has the wrong shape.
detail: In the Time-Frequency path begins_at (line 1415, via fliplr) and ends_at (lines 1416-1417) are 2-vectors [time freq]. Line 1430 `matched_data = tmp(:,begins_at:ends_at,:)` evaluates begins_at:ends_at as begins_at(1):ends_at(1), trimming only one dimension and collapsing the rest into the trailing colon. The subsequent assignment `data(:,:,:,:,matrix_index)=matched_data` (line 1441) expects a 4-D block, so it errors or stores mis-shaped data. Compare the correct 2-D form used in getdata (line 1994): `tmp(:,begins_at(1):ends_at(1),begins_at(2):ends_at(2),:)`.
failure_scenario: Repeated-measures ANOVA on ICA Components with LIMO.Analysis='Time-Frequency' and analysis_type='Full scalp analysis'. At line 1430 begins_at/ends_at are 2-vectors, so tmp(:,begins_at:ends_at,:) trims only the first TF axis and linearizes the remaining time and param dimensions into one, yielding a 3-D matched_data instead of 4-D. Line 1441 stores it into the 5-D data array as mis-shaped/corrupted data (the second TF-axis frame-matching is skipped and time/param axes are merged). The per-subject size check at line 1447 passes because every subject is mis-shaped identically, so no error is raised and corrupted data silently reaches limo_rep_anova, producing wrong results (or a downstream dimension crash when the collapsed array is reshaped/indexed).
minimal_fix: Branch on analysis type as in getdata (line 1994): replace line 1430 with `if strcmp(LIMO.Analysis,'Time-Frequency'); matched_data = tmp(:,begins_at(1):ends_at(1),begins_at(2):ends_at(2),:); else; matched_data = tmp(:,begins_at:ends_at,:); end`.
verifier_reasoning: Verified at lines 1414-1441. In the repeated-measures Full-scalp/Components branch, line 1430 `matched_data = tmp(:,begins_at:ends_at,:)` is used for ALL analysis types, but for Time-Frequency begins_at/ends_at are 2-element vectors (lines 1415-1417) and tmp is 4-D [chan,freq,time,param]. MATLAB's colon operator with vector operands silently uses only the first element, so begins_at:ends_at == begins_at(1):ends_at(1) — trimming only dim 2. With just three subscripts on a 4-D array, the trailing ':' linearizes dims 3 and 4, producing a 3-D result [chan, L, dim3*nparam]: the second TF axis is never trimmed and the time/param axes are collapsed together. Line 1441 then stores this into the 5-D slot data(:,:,:,:,matrix_index), yielding mis-shaped/corrupted data. Because line 1447's guard only compares subjects to each other (all identically mis-shaped), no error is raised there and the corruption silently flows into limo_rep_anova. The correct pattern exists in the getdata helper at line 1994, which explicitly branches on LIMO.Analysis and uses tmp(:,begins_at(1):ends_at(1),begins_at(2):ends_at(2),:). Line 1430 lacks that TF branch. Reachable with rep-measures ANOVA + Full scalp + Components + Time-Frequency, all valid configs. Not a duplicate of any known finding (none touch line 1430 or TF-Components trimming). Note the reporter's proposed fix must be guarded by a TF check, since line 1430 also correctly serves the non-TF Components case.

summary: The nargin==0 branch references EEG before it is ever defined, crashing the documented no-argument usage.
detail: When called with no arguments, line 30 executes `storein = EEG.filepath;` but EEG is never a parameter and is never loaded in this branch (only the nargin>=1 branch calls pop_loadset into EEG at line 32). The header comment claims the empty-input mode 'will read the EEG variable already in the workplace', but a MATLAB function cannot see base-workspace variables, so EEG is undefined in function scope. Execution errors immediately at line 30, and the subsequent EEG.event loop (lines 43-45) would fail identically. checkcode's NODEF flag on line 30 confirms this. The entire documented no-argument mode is non-functional.
failure_scenario: User invokes `limo_read_events` with zero arguments (the first documented FORMAT and the header-described empty-input mode intended to read the workspace EEG and write cat.mat). Execution reaches line 30 `storein = EEG.filepath;` where EEG has never been defined in function scope, so MATLAB throws 'Unrecognized function or variable EEG' and no cat.mat is produced. The mode is 100% non-functional.
minimal_fix: Make EEG available in the no-argument branch, e.g. `EEG = evalin('base','EEG'); storein = EEG.filepath;` if the intent is truly to pull the base-workspace EEG, or better, drop the no-arg mode and require the .set path (`EEG = pop_loadset(varargin{1})`) so EEG is always defined before the event loop.
verifier_reasoning: At line 30 the nargin==0 branch does `storein = EEG.filepath;`, but EEG is never a parameter (signature is `function cat = limo_read_events(varargin)`) and is only ever assigned inside the else branch via `pop_loadset(varargin{1})` at line 32. MATLAB function scope cannot access base-workspace variables, so EEG is undefined in this branch. Calling `limo_read_events` with no arguments — exactly the first documented FORMAT line and the header's `<empty>` mode — throws 'Unrecognized function or variable EEG' at line 30. The downstream `for n=1:size(EEG.event,2)` loop (lines 43-45) and `cat = NaN(size(EEG.event,2),1)` (line 51) would also fail on undefined EEG. The entire no-argument mode is dead. This is a real, deterministic crash reachable by the documented usage, not a wrong-numeric-result issue; hence medium rather than high given it is a peripheral helper and the .set-path mode is unaffected.

summary: `legacy_mode` is silently forced true for 2- and 3-argument calls, contradicting the documented default (false) and the 1-argument default (false).
detail: The header documents legacy_mode default = 'false'. The nargin dispatch sets legacy_mode=false only for nargin==1; nargin==2 (line 36) and nargin==3 (line 34) both hardcode legacy_mode=true. Since legacy vs non-legacy changes BOTH the Bayesian-bootstrap sampling distribution (exprnd at line 63 vs gamrnd at line 65) and the interval method (narrowest-interval HDI at lines 83-106 vs quantile indexing at lines 108-110), the statistical procedure silently flips depending purely on how many arguments the caller supplies. A user passing prob_coverage explicitly (3 args) gets a different method than one relying on the default (1 arg), with no way to know.
failure_scenario: Call limo_central_estimator(Y,'Mean',0.95) (nargin==3). The user reasonably expects the documented default legacy_mode=false, i.e. gamma-distributed Dirichlet weights (gamrnd) and quantile-based intervals. Instead line 35 sets legacy_mode=true, so the function uses exponential sampling (exprnd, line 63) and the narrowest-interval HDI construction (lines 83-106), producing different bootstrap weights and different interval endpoints than documented. The documented behavior is only obtained by either calling limo_central_estimator(Y) with no other arguments (nargin==1, line 42) — which additionally forces estimator='Trimmed mean' — or by explicitly appending legacy_mode=false as the 4th argument. Thus two calls the user believes both use the default method silently run different algorithms.
minimal_fix: Set legacy_mode=false in the nargin==2 and nargin==3 branches (change lines 35 and 38 to legacy_mode=false), so the documented default is honored whenever the caller does not explicitly pass legacy_mode; only a 4-argument call with legacy_mode=true selects the legacy path.
verifier_reasoning: The code exactly matches the report. Lines 34-43: nargin==1 sets legacy_mode=false, but nargin==2 (line 38) and nargin==3 (line 35) both hardcode legacy_mode=true. The header (line 11) documents default 'false' (gamma sampling + quantile intervals). Because legacy_mode gates BOTH the resampling distribution (exprnd line 63 vs gamrnd line 65) and the interval construction (narrowest-width HDI lines 83-106 vs quantile-index lines 108-110), any caller who supplies an explicit estimator and/or prob_coverage — but relies on the documented legacy default — silently receives the legacy algorithm. The documented non-legacy default is in fact only reachable via the single 1-argument call or by explicitly passing legacy_mode=false as a 4th argument. This is a genuine, reachable contradiction between documented and actual behavior that changes the statistical procedure and the returned intervals. It is not a crash and not a numerically catastrophic error (both paths are legitimate estimators), so severity is medium rather than high. Not a duplicate of the known line-109 finding, which is an unrelated indexing defect (unsorted bb) inside the non-legacy branch; different line, different root cause. Note the reported failure scenario is slightly imprecise: limo_central_estimator(Y) also forces estimator='Trimmed mean' (line 41), so the two example calls also differ in estimator, not only in legacy_mode. The corrected scenario isolates the legacy_mode flip.

summary: Loading a channel-vector .mat file uses `channel_vector.cell2mat(fieldname(channel_vector))`, which references the non-existent function `fieldname` and a bogus dynamic-field syntax, crashing the channel-optimized Betas/Con path.
detail: Line 538: `channel_vector = channel_vector.cell2mat(fieldname(channel_vector));`. `fieldname` is not a MATLAB function (the builtin is `fieldnames`), and `channel_vector.cell2mat(...)` is parsed as accessing a struct field/method named cell2mat rather than calling cell2mat on the fieldnames. The correct idiom, used elsewhere in this very file (lines 110, 321, 564), is `channel_vector.(cell2mat(fieldnames(channel_vector)))`.
failure_scenario: Call limo_central_tendency_and_ci with nargin such that this second-level Betas/Con file-selection branch runs. Select Analysis_type '1 channel only' at line 525. At the limo_inputdlg prompt (line 531) leave the channel entry empty so `isempty(cell2mat(channel))` is true (line 532). In the uigetfile dialog (line 533) pick any .mat file containing a channel-index vector so `index ~= 0`. load() at line 537 succeeds, producing a struct. Line 538 then evaluates `channel_vector.cell2mat(fieldname(channel_vector))`: MATLAB first tries to resolve `fieldname`, which does not exist, and throws "Unrecognized function or variable 'fieldname'". The analysis aborts before the channel restriction and data gathering. (Even if `fieldname` were corrected to `fieldnames`, the `channel_vector.cell2mat(...)` dot-access would then error because the struct has no field named `cell2mat`.)
minimal_fix: Replace line 538 with the dynamic-field idiom used elsewhere in the file: `channel_vector = channel_vector.(cell2mat(fieldnames(channel_vector)));`
verifier_reasoning: Line 538 is literally `channel_vector = channel_vector.cell2mat(fieldname(channel_vector));`. Two defects compound: (a) `fieldname` is not a MATLAB builtin (the correct name is `fieldnames`), so execution hits "Unrecognized function or variable 'fieldname'"; (b) even with the name fixed, `channel_vector.cell2mat(...)` is parsed as accessing a struct field literally named `cell2mat` (which does not exist on the loaded struct), not as a dynamic-field access. The correct idiom for pulling the single variable out of a loaded .mat struct is used verbatim elsewhere in this same file: line 110 `data = data.(cell2mat(fieldnames(data)));` and line 564 `Yr = Yr.(cell2mat(fieldnames(Yr)));`. The path is reachable: Betas/Con second-level path, Analysis_type '1 channel only' (line 530), user leaves the limo_inputdlg channel box empty so `isempty(cell2mat(channel))` is true (line 532), uigetfile returns a valid file with index~=0 (line 534), load succeeds (line 537), then line 538 crashes deterministically. This aborts the channel-optimized loading of a per-subject channel-index vector. Not a duplicate: known findings are at lines 360 (zero-pad estimator), 789 (size() non-scalar guard), and 1069 (TF Harrell-Davis init) — different lines and root causes. Severity medium: a guaranteed crash but confined to an optional, user-driven sub-path (empty channel box + file selection) rather than the default flow.

summary: In the ERP 1-channel path, `channel_vector.getfield(channel_vector)` passes the struct itself as the field-name argument to getfield, which crashes.
detail: Line 666: `channel_vector = channel_vector.getfield(channel_vector);`. getfield(S, FIELD) requires FIELD to be a char field name; here the struct is passed as the field name, so MATLAB errors ('Argument must be...' / invalid field). This is the ERP-branch analogue of the line 538 bug; the correct idiom is `channel_vector.(cell2mat(fieldnames(channel_vector)))`.
failure_scenario: Call limo_central_tendency_and_ci with nargin==1. Choose Raw Data (ERP) so execution enters the ERP branch at line 601. Select 3+ LIMO.mat files. At the 'type of analysis?' dialog choose '1 channel only' (line 657). At the 'which channel to analyse [?]' prompt leave it empty so `isempty(cell2mat(channel))` is true (line 659). At the uigetfile 'select your channel file' dialog pick a valid .mat containing the channel vector (so `file` is a char, isempty(file) false at line 661). Line 665 does `channel_vector = load(file)` (a struct); line 666 `channel_vector.getfield(channel_vector)` then throws 'Reference to non-existent field ''getfield''' (because the struct has no field named getfield), aborting the analysis before the channel vector is ever extracted.
minimal_fix: Replace line 666 with `channel_vector = channel_vector.(cell2mat(fieldnames(channel_vector)));` (matching the idiom already used at line 564), which extracts the loaded variable regardless of its saved name.
verifier_reasoning: Line 666 `channel_vector = channel_vector.getfield(channel_vector);` is reached in the nargin==1, ERP-analysis path (else at line 601) under Analysis_type '1 channel only' (line 657), when the channel input dialog is left empty (line 659) and a channel file is selected via uigetfile (line 660-665). `channel_vector` here is a struct from `load(file)`. For a plain struct, MATLAB dot-notation is field access, not method dispatch, so `channel_vector.getfield` attempts to read a field literally named 'getfield'. The loaded .mat variable is named after the saved channel-vector variable, not 'getfield', so this throws 'Reference to non-existent field ''getfield''' and aborts the analysis. (Even if the field somehow were named 'getfield', `(channel_vector)` would then index it with a struct subscript and still error.) This is a guaranteed crash on that path. The correct idiom, used elsewhere in the same file (e.g. line 564: `Yr = Yr.(cell2mat(fieldnames(Yr)));`), is dynamic field access. The report's core claim (crash + correct fix) is right; only the crash mechanism is mislabeled (non-existent-field error, not a getfield argument-type error). Not a duplicate: known findings are at 360 (zero-pad estimator), 789 (size scalar guard), 1069 (missing waitbar init); this is a distinct line/root cause, the ERP sibling of the separate (unlisted) line 538 bug. Severity medium: a hard crash but only on a specific interactive GUI path (nargin==1, ERP, single-channel, empty entry, file selected); it fails loudly rather than corrupting results.

summary: The within-subject 'Weighted Mean' computes mean(w.*x) over trials (divides by N), not the weighted mean sum(w.*x)/sum(w), biasing the estimate whenever trial weights do not sum to the number of trials.
detail: For Weighted Mean, tmp is filled with weighted data w_i*x_i at lines 357 (and 352-354 for TF, 736/800 in the ERP branch), then line 372/373 collapses trials with `mean(tmp,3)` = sum_i(w_i x_i)/N. A proper weighted mean is sum_i(w_i x_i)/sum_i(w_i). LIMO WLS trial weights lie in [0,1] and do not generally sum to N, so dividing by N systematically attenuates the estimate rather than producing the intended weighted average.
failure_scenario: A subject/condition with trial weights [1, 1, 0.2] and identical trial values [10, 10, 10]: the code computes w.*x = [10, 10, 2] and mean(...,3) = 22/3 = 7.33, whereas the correct weighted mean is sum(w.*x)/sum(w) = 22/2.2 = 10. The per-subject Weighted Mean estimate is attenuated by roughly (1 - mean(w)); with realistic WLS weights (most ~1, a few outliers near 0) this yields a systematic downward bias in ERP/TF amplitude that propagates into the group-level central tendency and its bootstrap CI.
minimal_fix: Give the Weighted Mean case its own collapse using the true weighted average instead of mean(tmp,3). Retain the raw weight tensor (e.g. wtmp(channel,:,:) = repmat of the trial weights over frames, aligned to the selected index) alongside the weighted-data tmp, then compute tmp = sum(tmp,3,'omitnan') ./ sum(wtmp,3,'omitnan') for the 'Weighted Mean' estimator (guarding sum(w)==0). Apply the same change to all four weighted branches (ERP ~357/373, TF ~352-354/373, and the group-level Evaluate/Pool branches at 736 and 800). Do not merge Weighted Mean with 'Mean' in the mean(tmp,3) line.
verifier_reasoning: The "Weighted Mean" estimator builds tmp = w_i·x_i (line 357 ERP, 352-354 TF, and the mirrored group-level branches at 736/800), then line 372/373 collapses trials with mean(tmp,3) = sum_i(w_i x_i)/N. The correct weighted mean is sum_i(w_i x_i)/sum_i(w_i). I confirmed via limo_WLS.m (weights come from limo_pcout, values in [0,1], outliers near 0) and limo_glm_handling.m:282 (W stored raw into LIMO.design.weights) that weights are NOT normalized to sum to N, so dividing by N systematically attenuates the estimate by the factor mean(w)=sum(w)/N. The weighted branch writes only selected trials into dim 3 (tmp(channel,:,:) = ...), so there is no zero-padding confound here — the bias is purely N vs sum(w), which is a different root cause from the known line-360 finding (zero-padding in the non-weighted tmp(channel,:,index) branch). Lines 789 and 1069 are unrelated. Hence a real, non-duplicate bug.

summary: 'cleaer boot_table' typo runs before varargout{1} is set, crashing even the implemented type-0 limo_trimci path
detail: Line 153 is `clear data; varargout{2} = boot_table; cleaer boot_table`. `cleaer` is not a MATLAB command/function, so command-syntax `cleaer boot_table` errors with 'Unrecognized function or variable cleaer'. Because this executes before line 154 (`varargout{1} = H0`), even the one fully-implemented branch (type 0 with test='limo_trimci') fails at the very end and never returns H0.
failure_scenario: Call the generic path, e.g. limo_compute_H0(0,data,nboot,'limo_trimci'). After also fixing the upstream blockers required to reach the end of the branch (line-38 `type = varargin(1)` cell indexing, the `varagin` typos on lines 45-46, and the undefined `parameter` used at line 136), the bootstrap over electrodes completes and H0 is built. Execution then hits line 153: `varargout{2} = boot_table` is assigned, but `cleaer boot_table` throws `Unrecognized function or variable 'cleaer'`, aborting before `varargout{1} = H0` on line 154. The function errors out and the caller never receives H0_data.
minimal_fix: Change `cleaer boot_table` to `clear boot_table` on line 153.
verifier_reasoning: Line 153 is `clear data; varargout{2} = boot_table; cleaer boot_table`. `cleaer` is not a MATLAB function/command; command-syntax `cleaer boot_table` resolves to a call of nonexistent function `cleaer` and throws `Unrecognized function or variable 'cleaer'`. This executes after `varargout{2}=boot_table` but before line 154 `varargout{1}=H0`, so H0 is never returned to the caller — a genuine crash. It is NOT a duplicate of the known line-38 finding (that is cell-indexing `type == 0`; this is a distinct misspelled `clear` on the return line, different line and root cause). Caveat on the reporter's framing: the type-0/limo_trimci branch is not actually 'fully implemented' — line 136 references `parameter`, which is never defined in the type-0 branch, so the branch crashes there before ever reaching 153 (in addition to the line-38 cell issue and the `varagin` typos on lines 45-46). So concretely reaching line 153 requires fixing several other independent bugs first; once they are fixed, line 153 reliably crashes. Real defect, correctly diagnosed, but lower practical impact in isolation since it is masked by multiple preceding blockers and is a one-character return-line typo — hence medium rather than high.

summary: Subject-count guard requires >=5 subjects but the error message says 'need at least 3', rejecting valid 4-subject datasets
detail: Line 49 `if size(data,3)-1 <= Nmin` with Nmin=3 aborts whenever subjects-1 <= 3, i.e. for 4 or fewer subjects, so it actually requires at least 5 subjects. The thrown message on line 50 says 'need at least 3 subjects', which is inconsistent with the actual threshold. A legitimate 4-subject second-level analysis is refused with a misleading message. (Line 42's low-count warning likewise hardcodes 3 instead of Nmin.)
failure_scenario: Call limo_create_boot_table(data,nboot) with data of size [channels x frames x 4] (4 subjects). Line 49 evaluates 4-1=3 <= Nmin(3) as true and throws error 'Not enough subjects in dataset - need at least 3 subjects', even though 4 >= 3. The valid 4-subject bootstrap is aborted with a message contradicting the actual enforced minimum of 5.
minimal_fix: Make the guard consistent with the message and with Nmin: replace line 49 with `if size(data,3) < Nmin` (rejects only <3 subjects) and keep the message tied to Nmin. Also replace the hardcoded `3` on line 42 with `Nmin` so the low-count warning matches.
verifier_reasoning: Verified against source. Line 26 sets Nmin=3; line 49 guards `if size(data,3)-1 <= Nmin`, i.e. errors whenever subjects-1 <= 3, i.e. subjects <= 4. So the function rejects any dataset with 4 or fewer subjects while line 50 tells the user 'need at least 3 subjects'. A 4-subject dataset satisfies the stated minimum yet is aborted — a real, reproducible spurious crash with a message that misstates the true (>=5) threshold. The downstream logic only requires Nmin unique draws per bootstrap (line 55 `length(unique(tmp)) >= Nmin`), which is achievable with 4 subjects, so the off-by-one rejection is not statistically justified. Line 42 independently hardcodes `<=3` instead of Nmin, reinforcing the inconsistency but not itself causing the crash. Severity medium: it blocks a legitimate small-N analysis with a wrong error rather than silently corrupting results.

summary: In case 3, a missing local LIMO.mat does not trigger the catch fallback, so LIMO is never defined and cd(LIMO.dir) crashes instead of prompting the user.
detail: The try block (lines 97-110) only loads LIMO when `exist(fullfile(pwd,'LIMO.mat'),'file')` is true. If no LIMO.mat exists in pwd, the if-body is skipped, the try completes with no error, and the catch (which runs the uigetfile prompt) is never entered. Execution falls through to line 111 `cd(LIMO.dir)` with LIMO undefined. The intended file-picker fallback only fires on a load error, not on a missing file, so the graceful path is dead.
failure_scenario: From MATLAB, `cd` to any directory that contains no LIMO.mat, then call `limo_eeg(3)`. Expected: the 'select a LIMO.mat file' uigetfile dialog appears (the coded fallback). Actual: the dialog never appears; the if-test at line 98 is false, the catch is skipped, and line 111 `cd(LIMO.dir)` throws 'Unrecognized function or variable LIMO'.
minimal_fix: Move the file-picker into an else branch of the exist check instead of relying on catch, e.g.: `if exist(fullfile(pwd,'LIMO.mat'),"file"); load(fullfile(pwd,'LIMO.mat')); else; [file,filepath]=uigetfile('LIMO.mat','select a LIMO.mat file'); if isequal(file,0); return; else; load(fullfile(filepath,file)); end; end`. Keep a try/catch around the load to still cover a corrupt-file error, but ensure a missing local file drives the uigetfile prompt.
verifier_reasoning: In case 3, LIMO is a function-local variable (no `global LIMO`; verified) set only by the load() calls at lines 101/108. The try block (97-110) loads LIMO only when `exist(fullfile(pwd,'LIMO.mat'),"file")` is true. When no local LIMO.mat exists, the if-condition is false, the body is skipped, the try exits normally, and the catch (which runs the uigetfile fallback) is never entered because catch fires only on a thrown error, not on a false if-test. Execution then reaches line 111 `cd(LIMO.dir); %#ok<NODEF>` with LIMO undefined, crashing with 'Unrecognized function or variable LIMO'. The `%#ok<NODEF>` suppression comment corroborates that the Code Analyzer already flags LIMO as possibly undefined here. The intended graceful file-picker fallback is dead for the missing-file case. Concretely reachable via the documented `limo_eeg(3)` shortcut. Not a duplicate: no known finding covers line 111 or this try/catch missing-file logic (:405 concerns a different case-4 filename comparison). Severity medium rather than the reported high: it is a hard crash with a clear message but confined to manual/interactive invocation in a directory lacking LIMO.mat (the normal batch/GUI flow cd's into the dir holding a just-saved LIMO.mat, so the if-branch runs); no statistical result is silently corrupted.

summary: Multivariate contrast results are gated on strfind for lowercase 'multivariate', but type_of_analysis is 'Multivariate', so the block never runs and multivariate contrast results are never stored.
detail: `if strfind(LIMO.design.type_of_analysis,'multivariate')` is case-sensitive. The only values assigned to type_of_analysis are 'Mass-univariate' and 'Multivariate' (lines 419/427). Neither contains the lowercase substring 'multivariate' ('Mass-univariate' contains 'univariate'; 'Multivariate' differs by capital M). strfind therefore returns [] for both, and `if []` is false, so `LIMO.contrast{...}.multivariate = result` is never executed. Multivariate contrasts computed by limo_contrast are silently discarded.
failure_scenario: On a LIMO directory with LIMO.design.type_of_analysis == 'Multivariate', run limo_eeg(6,C). For each contrast, limo_contrast(Yr,Betas,LIMO,0,1) computes a multivariate result, but the case-sensitive strfind guard at line 832 is always false ('Multivariate' has no lowercase 'multivariate' substring), so LIMO.contrast{...}.multivariate is never written. The result is silently discarded and any downstream display of multivariate contrast statistics finds nothing.
minimal_fix: Replace the case-sensitive guard with a case-insensitive exact test: `if strcmpi(LIMO.design.type_of_analysis,'Multivariate')`. (If substring matching is truly intended, use `~isempty(strfind(lower(LIMO.design.type_of_analysis),'multivariate'))` or `contains(LIMO.design.type_of_analysis,'multivariate','IgnoreCase',true)`.) Also note the store should use previous_con+i (the separately-known line-833 defect).
verifier_reasoning: At limo_eeg.m:832 the guard is `if strfind(LIMO.design.type_of_analysis,'multivariate')`. strfind is case-sensitive. The only assignments to type_of_analysis are 'Mass-univariate' (419) and 'Multivariate' (427). 'Mass-univariate' contains 'univariate' but not 'multivariate'; 'Multivariate' differs from the pattern by the capital M. strfind returns [] in both cases, and `if []` evaluates false, so LIMO.contrast{...}.multivariate = result is never assigned. Multivariate contrasts computed by limo_contrast at line 829 are silently discarded. Concrete input: a LIMO directory whose LIMO.design.type_of_analysis == 'Multivariate', calling limo_eeg(6,C) — result is computed each iteration but never stored, so any downstream code/display reading LIMO.contrast{i}.multivariate finds nothing. This is a real correctness/data-loss defect. It is distinct from the known line-833 finding, which concerns the index (i vs previous_con+i) used to store the result; that critique presumes the block runs, whereas this defect is that the block never runs at all. Different root cause and different line, so not a duplicate. Severity medium: silent loss of results confined to the less-common multivariate-contrast path, no crash.

summary: dir('AN(C)OVA*') treats the parentheses literally, so it never matches the real 'ANOVA'/'ANCOVA' folders and dirContent is always empty.
detail: Line 21 does dirContent = dir('AN(C)OVA*'). MATLAB's dir only honors * and ? as wildcards; every other character, including '(' and ')', is matched literally. The clear intent is a regex-style 'match ANOVA or ANCOVA', but as written the pattern only matches names beginning with the literal characters 'AN(C)OVA'. LIMO creates folders named 'ANOVA', 'ANCOVA', 'Rep_Meas_ANOVA' (see limo_random_effect.m update_dir calls), none of which contain '(C)'. Therefore dirContent is always empty, and the function always falls into the isempty(dirContent) branch (line 23) presenting the 'you need to run an ANOVA first' dialog even when valid ANOVA result folders exist. The automatic ANOVA-folder detection/selection path (lines 41-55) is effectively dead.
failure_scenario: User has a valid 'ANOVA' (or 'ANCOVA'/'Rep_Meas_ANOVA') result folder and invokes limo_get_anova_files for a 2nd-level contrast. Because dir('AN(C)OVA*') matches nothing, dirContent is empty, so instead of auto-listing/selecting the existing ANOVA folder (lines 41-55, now dead), the function always presents the fallback questdlg. The user can still recover manually via 'Pick ANOVA folder' (uigetdir), but the intended automatic detection never works; if they instead pick 'Run 1st-level contrast' they hit the separate nonexistent-function crash.
minimal_fix: Replace the single literal glob with real patterns and concatenate: dirContent = [dir('ANOVA*'); dir('ANCOVA*'); dir('Rep_Meas_ANOVA*')]; or filter dir('*') with regexp on '^(Rep_Meas_)?ANC?OVA'.
verifier_reasoning: Line 21 does dirContent = dir('AN(C)OVA*'). In MATLAB's dir(), only * and ? are wildcards; every other character, including the parentheses, is matched literally. The pattern therefore only matches directory names literally beginning with the 8 characters AN(C)OVA. Verified in the codebase that LIMO creates result folders named 'ANOVA', 'ANCOVA', 'ANOVA2', 'Rep_Meas_ANOVA' (limo_random_effect.m:319-326 update_dir calls), none of which contain the substring '(C)'. Consequently dirContent is always empty, the isempty branch (line 23) always fires, and the automatic ANOVA-folder detection/selection block (lines 41-55) is unreachable dead code. The reported root cause and mechanism are correct. This is a distinct defect from the already-known line-31 finding (nonexistent limo_get_result_files): different line, different root cause. Note that the empty-dirContent path still offers a manual 'Pick ANOVA folder' (uigetdir) escape hatch at lines 33-38, and does not itself crash, corrupt data, or produce a wrong statistical result — it is a workflow/UX degradation that defeats auto-detection and the multi-folder chooser. Hence medium rather than high. The report's failure narrative slightly overstates: reaching the nonexistent-function crash requires the user to additionally choose 'Run 1st-level contrast' (line 30-31), which is the separately-tracked bug.

summary: HC4 branch stores the parameter variance (diagonal of the sandwich covariance) into betas_se without taking the square root, so reported standard errors are variances, not standard errors.
detail: At line 283 the HC4 option computes `model.betas_se(:,t) = diag((pinv(WX'*WX))*WX'*diag(HC4(:,t))*WX*(pinv(WX'*WX)))`, i.e. the diagonal of the heteroskedasticity-consistent covariance matrix, with no sqrt. Every other betas_se computation in the file takes a square root: the standard OLS/WLS branch (line 285, `sqrt(...)`) and the IRLS branch (line 989, `sqrt(diag((E/dfe)*pinv(WX'*WX)))`). Since the field is named betas_se (standard error), the HC4 path returns squared units (variances), which is wrong for any downstream use as a standard error (e.g. t = beta/betas_se).
failure_scenario: Call limo_glm with the HC4 variance option enabled (variance_estimates='HC4' via the appropriate varargin slot) under OLS or WLS with any valid Y/X. Line 232 fills HC4; the loop at 281-288 hits line 283, which assigns the diagonal of the HC4 sandwich covariance (parameter variances) to model.betas_se without sqrt. The returned model.betas_se holds variances rather than standard errors, so any t-like ratio beta/betas_se is off by a factor equal to the SE itself (e.g., a true SE of 0.25 is reported as 0.0625, inflating t by 4x).
minimal_fix: Wrap the diagonal in sqrt at line 283: model.betas_se(:,t) = sqrt(diag((pinv(WX'*WX))*WX'*diag(HC4(:,t))*WX*(pinv(WX'*WX))));
verifier_reasoning: Line 283 (OLS/WLS HC4 branch) computes model.betas_se(:,t) = diag(bread*meat*bread) where bread=pinv(WX'*WX) and meat=WX'*diag(HC4(:,t))*WX. That diagonal is the heteroskedasticity-consistent parameter VARIANCE, and no sqrt is applied. Every other betas_se computation in the file takes a square root: the sibling else branch at 285 (sqrt(...)) and the IRLS branch at 989 (sqrt(diag(...))). Since the field is named betas_se (standard error), the HC4 path returns squared units, so any downstream ratio t = beta/betas_se is wrong (understated by a factor of sqrt(variance)). The path is reachable: variance_estimates is an input (varargin{3} or {10}) equal to 'HC4', and HC4 is populated at line 232 before the loop, so line 283 runs without error and silently corrupts the SE. This is a genuine correctness defect, not a crash. NOT a duplicate: the known 285 finding concerns the standard (else) branch producing a bogus scalar pooled-df SE — different branch, different root cause (that branch does take sqrt but uses a wrong formula). The known 599 finding is the separate TF (3-D) HC4 line that crashes on invalid indexing/syntax; line 283 is the 2-D OLS/WLS path and executes cleanly, just with the wrong (un-square-rooted) value. Severity medium: it is an opt-in, undocumented variance option and betas_se does not feed the main F/R2 tests (those use E/dfe independently), but the returned field is numerically wrong for any user relying on it as a standard error.

summary: Harrell-Davis weights are computed for the full length n but then subset by non-NaN mask, yielding wrong order-statistic weights (that no longer sum to 1) whenever NaNs are present.
detail: The weight vector w (line 50) is `betacdf(vec./n,...)-betacdf((vec-1)./n,...)` with vec=1:n and n=size(data,3) (full third-dim length, NaNs included). At lines 57 and 96 the estimate is `sum(w(~isnan(tmp_data))'.*tmp_data(~isnan(tmp_data)))`. When tmp_data contains k NaNs, only n-k weights are selected, but those weights were derived for an n-length sample. HD weights depend on the effective sample size, so the retained weights neither sum to 1 nor correspond to the correct order-statistic positions of the reduced sample -> biased quantile estimate. Additionally the <=10-observation guard (line 70) uses the NaN-inclusive count, so a slice with many NaNs can proceed with too few valid points.
failure_scenario: Call HD=limo_harrell_davis(data,0.5) with data of size [1 x 1 x 30] where 5 entries are NaN. n=30, so w (length 30) is built for a 30-sample HD. After sort(data,3) the 25 finite values occupy positions 1:25 (ascending) and the 5 NaNs occupy 26:30. w(~isnan) therefore keeps w(1:25), which sum to betacdf(25/30,15.5,15.5)≈0.86 (<1) and correspond to ranks of a 30-sample rather than the 25 valid observations, so HD(1,1) is a biased estimate of the requested 0.5 quantile. With option=1 (nboot given) the same bias hits HD(i,j,2) at line 96, and if e.g. data is [1x1x12] with 4 NaNs the size(data,3)=12>10 guard passes even though only 8 valid points remain.
minimal_fix: Recompute the HD weights per slice from the count of valid observations instead of subsetting the full-length w. Inside the i,j loop: nn=sum(~isnan(tmp_data)); vec=1:nn; m1=(nn+1)*q; m2=(nn+1)*(1-q); ww=betacdf(vec./nn,m1,m2)-betacdf((vec-1)./nn,m1,m2); then HD(i,j)=sum(ww'.*tmp_data(~isnan(tmp_data))). Apply the same at line 96, and change the line-70 minimum-observation guard to test the per-slice valid count (nn>10) rather than size(data,3).
verifier_reasoning: Line 48 sets n=size(data,3) (full third-dim length, NaN-inclusive) and line 50 builds HD weights w for an n-observation sample: w(i)=betacdf(i/n,(n+1)q,(n+1)(1-q))-betacdf((i-1)/n,...), which telescope to sum=1 over i=1..n. Line 51 sorts ascending (sort places NaNs LAST), so per slice ~isnan(tmp_data) is true for the first k sorted valid values, and line 57/96 select w(1:k). This subset sums to betacdf(k/n,a,b)-0 < 1 and is anchored to the wrong effective sample size; the correct HD for k valid observations needs weights recomputed with n->k, vec=1:k. Hence a biased quantile estimate whenever a slice contains NaNs. The ~isnan masking is deliberately coded, confirming NaN input is an intended path, so this is genuine mis-handling, not an unreachable branch. The same defect repeats at line 96 (CI path), and the >10-observation guard at line 70 uses the NaN-inclusive size(data,3), letting slices with too few valid points proceed. Not a duplicate of the known line-80 finding, which concerns the CI constant c selection (q<=2||q>=8 decile-index thresholds vs q being a fraction) — a different root cause and different code region. Severity medium: real statistical bias but only manifests when NaNs are present in the third dimension and only in the robust HD/quantile path.

summary: Escoufier/RV effect-size denominator uses element-wise `.^2` instead of the matrix square, so model.R2.V drops all off-diagonal covariance and is inflated
detail: The generalized R2 (Robert & Escoufier RV coefficient) is computed as `trace(Sxy*Syx) / sqrt(trace(Sxx.^2)*trace(Syy.^2))`. The numerator uses genuine matrix products, but the denominator uses `Sxx.^2` / `Syy.^2`, which is ELEMENT-WISE squaring. `trace(Sxx.^2)` sums only the squared diagonal entries (sum_i Sxx(i,i)^2), whereas the RV coefficient requires `trace(Sxx*Sxx)` = sum over ALL i,j of Sxx(i,j)^2 (the squared Frobenius norm). Whenever the regressors or the electrodes are correlated (nonzero off-diagonal covariances — essentially always), the denominator is too small and Rsquare_multi (reported as model.R2.V) is systematically inflated and can exceed 1. This is the same mistake in all four branch copies (lines 180, 324, 463, 801) and is unrelated to the already-reported eigenvector-capture bug.
failure_scenario: Any multivariate design, e.g. a MANOVA on 3+ correlated EEG channels (Y has nonzero off-diagonal channel covariance) with a 2-condition or covariate design (Sxx non-diagonal): trace(Sxx.^2)<trace(Sxx*Sxx) and trace(Syy.^2)<trace(Syy*Syy), so the denominator sqrt(trace(Sxx.^2)*trace(Syy.^2)) is too small and model.R2.V is systematically larger than the true RV coefficient, potentially >1. F/p values are unaffected because they derive from Eigen_values_R2.
minimal_fix: Replace the element-wise squarings with matrix squares at lines 180, 324, 463, 801: use trace(Sxx*Sxx) and trace(Syy*Syy) (equivalently Sxx^2 / Syy^2) in the denominator, i.e. Rsquare_multi = trace(Sxy*Syx)/sqrt(trace(Sxx*Sxx)*trace(Syy*Syy)). This is exact for scalar Sxx too, so no special-casing needed.
verifier_reasoning: Line 180 (and copies 324/463/801) compute the Robert & Escoufier RV generalized R2 as trace(Sxy*Syx)/sqrt(trace(Sxx.^2)*trace(Syy.^2)). The `.^2` is element-wise, so trace(Sxx.^2)=sum_i Sxx(i,i)^2 (only squared diagonal), whereas the RV coefficient needs trace(Sxx*Sxx)=sum_{i,j}Sxx(i,j)^2 (squared Frobenius norm). For any symmetric S with nonzero off-diagonals, trace(S.^2)<trace(S*S), so the denominator is understated while the numerator uses genuine matrix products, inflating Rsquare_multi (can exceed 1). Y is multivariate EEG data whose channel covariance Syy is essentially always non-diagonal, so the bug fires on virtually every MANOVA call; correlated regressors make Sxx non-diagonal too. Confirmed reachable: model.R2.V = Rsquare_multi (line 864) exports the wrong value. It is a descriptive effect-size only; the Roy/Pillai F and p come from Eigen_values_R2, not this quantity, so no test statistic is corrupted -> medium, not high. Not a duplicate: all known limo_mglm findings concern different lines/root causes (eigenvector capture at 182, undefined weight vars, missing repamt, etc.); none touch the RV denominator at 180.

summary: On the bootstrap error/fallback path the code cd's into 'H0' but never cd's back, leaving the working directory corrupted
detail: The pattern `try cd('H0');load(MCC_data); cd ..` (lines 80, 154, 225) changes into the H0 subdirectory before loading. If `load(MCC_data)` fails (bootstrap file absent) or any statement before `cd ..` throws (e.g. the already-reported size(M,2) loop, or U rounding to 0), control jumps to the catch block WITHOUT executing `cd ..`. The catch computes the theoretical-threshold fallback and returns, but the process is left with its current directory inside 'H0'. The caller (limo_display_results) then loads/saves subsequent files relative to the wrong directory, causing file-not-found errors or writes to the wrong location.
failure_scenario: Call limo_display_results on a multivariate R2.mat with LIMO.design.bootstrap==1, MCC==1, and an H0 subfolder that exists (bootstrap dir present) but whose H0_R2.mat is missing or unreadable. Line 80 `cd('H0')` succeeds; `load(MCC_data)` throws; control jumps to the catch at line 94, which builds the Roy/Pillai fallback mask/M and returns — but never runs `cd ..`, so the MATLAB process cwd is now `.../H0`. The same corruption also occurs even when the file loads, if `U=round((1-p)*N)` rounds to 0 and `sorted_values(:,U)` throws inside the try. After return, the next relative file operation in the toolbox (e.g. `load('Betas.mat')` in limo_display_results) resolves against `.../H0/Betas.mat` and fails with file-not-found, or a later save writes to the wrong directory.
minimal_fix: Do not rely on cwd. Replace `try cd('H0');load(MCC_data); cd ..` with an absolute-path load that never changes directory: `try load(fullfile(LIMO.dir,'H0',MCC_data)); ...`. If a directory change must be kept, capture and restore it in the catch: `oldpwd = pwd; try cd('H0'); load(MCC_data); cd(oldpwd); ... catch ME; cd(oldpwd); ... end`, or guard with `c = onCleanup(@() cd(oldpwd));`. Apply to all three sites (lines 80, 154, 225 and their catch blocks 94, 168, 239).
verifier_reasoning: The pattern `try cd('H0'); load(MCC_data); cd ..` at lines 80, 154, 225 restores the working directory (`cd ..`) only on the success path. If `cd('H0')` succeeds (the H0 folder exists because bootstrap was run) but any statement before `cd ..` throws — e.g. `load(MCC_data)` fails because `H0_R2.mat`/`H0_Condition_effect.mat` is absent or corrupt inside an existing H0 dir, or `U = round((1-p)*size(sorted_values,2))` rounds to 0 so `sorted_values(:,U)` throws an index error, or the already-known `size(M,2)` loop mis-indexes — control jumps to the `catch ME` block (94/168/239). That catch computes the theoretical-threshold fallback and returns WITHOUT any `cd ..`, so cwd is left process-globally stuck inside `H0`. This is a real, verifiable defect: `cd` mutates global process state, not a scoped variable, and there is no onCleanup guard. The downstream impact is confirmed: the caller limo_display_results.m performs relative loads (`load('Betas.mat')` at lines 1652/1660/1726/1737) that assume cwd==LIMO.dir, so after the corrupted return those resolve against `.../H0/Betas.mat` and fail, and any subsequent GUI/display operation in the session inherits the wrong cwd. Root cause (unrestored cd on catch) is distinct from all three known findings, which live inside the try body (line 89 loop dimensionality, line 120 empty MCC 2-5 branches, line 214 Covariate reading R2/H0_R2 variable-name), so not a duplicate. Severity medium rather than high because triggering requires the bootstrap error path (missing/corrupt H0 file or degenerate U), not the normal flow; but when triggered it silently corrupts session state and causes later file-not-found or misdirected reads.

summary: differences_Callback deletes handles.figure1 then keeps operating on hObject (a child of that figure), causing an invalid-handle error.
detail: Inside the `if go` block, line 105 does delete(handles.figure1), which destroys the GUI figure and all its child uicontrols, including hObject (the differences button that fired the callback). After the block, line 107 calls set(hObject,'Visible','off'), then line 109 set(hObject,'Visible','on'), and lines 110/114 guidata(hObject,handles). All of these operate on the now-deleted hObject and throw 'Invalid or deleted object'. Contrast with Central_tendency_and_CI_Callback, which deletes figure1 and then returns without touching hObject. The delete plus subsequent set/guidata on the same handle is contradictory.
failure_scenario: User sets Analysis type to Channels with valid chanlocs loaded (so test_chan_loc returns go=1), and handles.dir already set (or update_dir succeeds), then clicks the differences button. uiresume, guidata(hObject,handles), delete(handles.figure1), and limo_central_tendency_and_ci(handles.chan_file) all execute. After the if-go block, line 107 set(hObject,'Visible','off') runs on hObject, a child of the just-deleted figure1, throwing 'Invalid or deleted object handle'. The callback aborts before limo_plot_difference (line 108) can run, so the difference plot is never produced.
minimal_fix: Do not delete the figure on this path, or move the button-toggle/plot logic before the delete. Simplest: remove delete(handles.figure1) at line 104 so hObject stays valid for the set/guidata calls; alternatively mirror Central_tendency_and_CI_Callback by dropping the post-delete set(hObject,...)/guidata references and calling limo_plot_difference before the delete.
verifier_reasoning: Verified at /Users/devon7y/VS_Code/limo_tools/limo_random_effect.m:91-114. In differences_Callback, when handles.type=='Channels' and go=test_chan_loc(handles) is true, line 104 delete(handles.figure1) destroys the figure and all child uicontrols, including hObject (the differences button that fired this callback). Line 103 guidata(hObject,handles) runs before the delete, but line 107 set(hObject,'Visible','off') runs after the if-go block unconditionally, on the now-deleted handle, throwing 'Invalid or deleted object'. Lines 108-110/114 (limo_plot_difference, Visible on, guidata) are never reached. The sibling Central_tendency_and_CI_Callback (lines 67-86) does the same delete but correctly returns without further hObject use, confirming the intended pattern and that this path is a genuine defect.
