summary: ANOVA_Callback has a duplicated inner `if strcmpi(handles.type,'Channels')`, making its else unreachable and leaving Components/Sources with no handler, so non-channel ANOVA silently does nothing.
detail: Line 334 opens `if strcmpi(handles.type,'Channels')` and line 335 immediately repeats the same test. Because the inner test can only be reached when the outer test was already true, the inner else (lines 340-341, which calls limo_random_select(answer,[],...) for non-channel data) is unreachable dead code. Meanwhile the outer if has no else at all, so when handles.type is 'Components' or 'Sources' the whole body is skipped and the ANOVA does nothing and gives no message. This differs from the t-test callbacks (e.g. One_Sample_t_test_Callback lines 203-210) which correctly branch Channels vs non-Channels.
failure_scenario: User selects analysis type Components (or Sources) in the limo_random_effect GUI (setting handles.type='Components'), clicks ANOVA, and picks any model (N-Ways ANOVA / ANCOVA / Repeated Measures). update_dir returns go~=0. Execution reaches line 334; `strcmpi('Components','Channels')` is false; the outer if has no else, so the function falls through to line 344 and returns. limo_random_select is never called: no ANOVA is computed, no file written, and no error or warning is displayed. The button appears to do nothing.
minimal_fix: Replace the duplicated inner if/else with a proper outer else, mirroring the t-test callbacks. In the `else` branch of `if go == 0` (lines 334-343): `if strcmpi(handles.type,'Channels'); go = test_chan_loc(handles); if go; limo_random_select(answer,handles.chan_file,'nboot',handles.b,'tfce',handles.tfce,'type',handles.type); end; else; limo_random_select(answer,[],'nboot',handles.b,'tfce',handles.tfce,'type',handles.type); end` — i.e. delete the redundant inner `if strcmpi(handles.type,'Channels')` (line 335) and its matching `end`, and attach the non-channel limo_random_select call to the outer if's else.
verifier_reasoning: Verified against source. Lines 334-343 of ANOVA_Callback contain `if strcmpi(handles.type,'Channels')` immediately nested inside an identical `if strcmpi(handles.type,'Channels')`. The inner else (line 340-341, the non-channel branch calling limo_random_select(answer,[],...)) is only reachable when the outer test is already true, i.e. never — it is dead code. The outer if has NO else. handles.type is genuinely 'Components' (line 180) or 'Sources' (line 182) via the type selector; default is 'Channels' (line 47). So for Components/Sources, after a valid model is chosen and go~=0, the entire body at line 334 is skipped: no limo_random_select call, no error, no message — a silent no-op. This contrasts with the sibling callbacks (One_Sample 203-210, Two_Samples 233-239, Paired 262-268, Regression 290-298) which all correctly branch Channels-vs-else. This is a real functional defect, not merely stylistic. Not a duplicate: the only known finding here is the line 148 num2bouble typo in bootstrap_Callback — different function, different root cause. Severity medium (not high) because it is confined to non-channel (ICA component / source) second-level ANOVA workflows, produces no crash or wrong statistical output/corruption, and channel-based analyses (the common case and default) are unaffected; the harm is a silently non-functional button for a minority workflow.

summary: Two-samples t-test result filename uses a format string with two %g specifiers but passes only the single argument `parameter`, producing a malformed name with a dangling underscore when `parameter` is scalar.
detail: Line 337 `name = sprintf('Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_%g_%g',parameter);` and line 368 `boot_name = sprintf('Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_%g_%g_desc-H0',parameter);` both have two `%g` conversions but only one value. When `parameter` is a scalar, sprintf consumes it for the first `%g` and stops at the second (no argument left), yielding e.g. 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_5_' with a trailing underscore and no second value. The paired-test code (line 506) correctly handles this with `sprintf('..._%d_%d',parameter(1),parameter(end))`. Callers `limo_results.m:312` and `limo_display_results.m:149` pass a SCALAR (`str2double(...)`/`str2num(...)` of a single trailing token), so the malformed name is reachable in normal use. The result file and its H0 file are then written under the truncated name, breaking any downstream code/display that expects the canonical 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_X' filename.
failure_scenario: In the results GUI, select an existing two-samples result file 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_1_2.mat' and trigger a recompute (limo_results.m:286 → 311-313). fileparts strips '.mat', and str2num('2') (token after the last underscore) passes the scalar parameter=2 into limo_random_robust case 2. At line 337, sprintf('Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_%g_%g',2) returns 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_2_' (MATLAB stops before the unmatched second %g). Line 360 saves the result under 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_2_.mat', and at line 368 boot_name becomes 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_2_' (both the second value AND the '_desc-H0' suffix are dropped), so the H0 file is written as 'H0/Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_2_.mat'. Subsequent display/lookup for the canonical 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_1_2' (and its '_desc-H0' H0 counterpart) fails to find the files. limo_display_results.m:149-150 exhibits the identical malformed-name behavior.
minimal_fix: Guard the scalar case as the paired-test code does. Replace line 337 with: `if isscalar(parameter), name = sprintf('Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_%g',parameter); else, name = sprintf('Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_%g_%g',parameter(1),parameter(end)); end` and apply the same isscalar guard to boot_name at line 368 (single %g when scalar, else parameter(1)/parameter(end)). More robustly, also fix the callers (limo_results.m:313, limo_display_results.m:150) to parse BOTH parameter tokens like the paired branch at limo_display_results.m:152-156, so the recompute reproduces the original two-parameter name.
verifier_reasoning: Line 337 (`sprintf('Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_%g_%g',parameter)`) and line 368 (boot_name) each contain two %g specifiers but pass the single variable `parameter`. This is only well-formed when `parameter` is a 2-element vector. The primary computation path (limo_random_select.m:589) always passes a 2-element `[p1 p2]` (enforced at limo_random_select.m:504-508), so first-run naming is correct. However the GUI recompute path is genuinely reachable with a SCALAR: limo_results.m:286 strips the extension with fileparts, then line 313 passes str2num(filename after last '_') — for an existing canonical file 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_1_2' this yields the scalar 2. limo_display_results.m:149-150 does the same. With a scalar, per documented MATLAB sprintf semantics the format is emitted only up to (not including) the unmatched second %g: name becomes 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_2_' and boot_name becomes 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_2_' (the trailing '_desc-H0' literal is also dropped). The result .mat (line 360) and the H0 file (~line 430) are written under these dangling-underscore names, so any downstream lookup/plot expecting the canonical 'Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_...' name fails, and the H0 file loses its '_desc-H0' tag entirely. This is a real implementation defect, not a duplicate: the only other two-samples finding at line 351 is a distinct statistical SE bug. Severity is medium rather than high because it manifests only on the recompute path (first-run naming is correct), and that same recompute path is already degraded (it passes Yr1.mat/Y1r.mat as BOTH groups), limiting practical exposure.

summary: When the tfce file already exists (e.g. user chooses 'overwrite'), the F/other branch dereferences thresholded_maps that was never assigned, crashing with an undefined-variable error.
detail: The observed-data TFCE is computed only inside if ~isfile(tfce_file) (line 255). If tfce_file already exists, that block is skipped and the output variable thresholded_maps is never set. In the F/other branch, lines 316-317 then unconditionally execute tmp = thresholded_maps; thresholded_maps{1} = tmp, referencing the unset variable -> 'Undefined function or variable thresholded_maps'. This is reachable via limo_results.m:352 (checkfile 'yes'): answering 'Yes' to the overwrite prompt sets LIMO.design.tfce=1 but does NOT delete the existing tfce_file, so ~isfile(tfce_file) is false and computation is skipped, leading straight to the crash. The R2 and t-test branches have the same latent issue at lines 178 and 247 but only when an H0 file also exists; the F branch crashes unconditionally.
failure_scenario: Run limo_tfce_handling on an F-type stat file (e.g. an ANOVA Condition_effect / ess file) whose tfce output file already exists on disk. Two concrete entries: (a) call with 'checkfile','yes' and answer "Yes" at the overwrite questdlg — this sets LIMO.design.tfce=1 but does not delete the existing tfce_file; or (b) call with 'checkfile','no'. Either way, at line 255 `~isfile(tfce_file)` is false, so the observed-data TFCE block (which is the only place thresholded_maps is set) is skipped. Execution falls through the optional H0 block to line 316 `tmp = thresholded_maps;`, which references the never-assigned output variable and errors with "Undefined function or variable 'thresholded_maps'" instead of overwriting the file. So a user choosing to overwrite/re-run TFCE on an F result crashes.
minimal_fix: Guard the cell-assembly so it only runs when thresholded_maps was actually computed, e.g. wrap lines 316-320 in `if exist('thresholded_maps','var')`. The robust fix is to actually honor overwrite: when the user answers "Yes" (or checkfile='no'), delete/recompute by removing the stale tfce_file before the `if ~isfile(tfce_file)` check (e.g. `delete(tfce_file)` in the "Yes" branch at line 79-81), so the observed-data block recomputes thresholded_maps as intended. Apply the same exist-guard to the R2 (178) and t-test (247) branches for consistency.
verifier_reasoning: In the F/other branch, thresholded_maps is assigned only inside the `if ~isfile(tfce_file)` block (lines 264/266/270/272). Line 316 `tmp = thresholded_maps` runs unconditionally (it sits after the H0 block, and only line 318's tfce_H0_thmaps is exist-guarded). When tfce_file already exists on disk, line 255 skips the observed-data computation, leaving thresholded_maps unassigned; because it is an output argument MATLAB does not auto-initialize it, so line 316 throws "Undefined function or variable 'thresholded_maps'". The overwrite path is real: lines 77-85 set LIMO.design.tfce=1 on a "Yes" answer but never delete the existing tfce_file, so ~isfile(tfce_file) is false. Also reachable directly with checkfile='no' when the file pre-exists. The R2/t-test branches (178/247) have the same latent flaw but are shielded because their `tmp = thresholded_maps` lines sit inside `if exist(H0filename,'file')`; the F branch line 316 is unguarded and crashes whenever the tfce_file exists. Not a duplicate of the three known findings (types 2-vs-3 at 303, type 1-vs-2 at 289, parfor whole-variable capture at 153) — different root cause (undefined variable on the skip/overwrite path). Severity medium: a crash, not silent corruption; only triggers on re-running/overwriting TFCE for an existing result, and is recoverable by deleting the stale tfce file.

summary: The ess-file trimming Fval = Fval(:,:,end-1:end) uses 3-D indexing that is wrong for 4-D Time-Frequency ess data, collapsing the freq/time axes.
detail: For ess (contrast) files the code trims to the last two stat pages via Fval = Fval(:,:,end-1:end). This assumes Fval is 3-D [chan x time x npages]. For Time-Frequency data an ess Fval is 4-D [chan x freq x time x npages]; the 3-subscript form (:,:,end-1:end) linearizes dimensions 3 and 4 together and selects the last two columns of that flattened axis, producing a corrupted 3-D array instead of retaining freq x time. The subsequent size(Fval,1)==1 / Time-Frequency branch then calls squeeze(Fval(:,:,:,1)) on the mangled array, feeding wrong data (or wrong shape) into limo_tfce.
failure_scenario: Run a 2nd-level repeated-measures or N-way ANOVA contrast (limo_contrast) on Time-Frequency data with LIMO.design.tfce enabled. The observed ess_1.mat is saved 4-D [chan x freq x time x 5]. limo_tfce_handling reaches the else-branch, and line 259 Fval=Fval(:,:,end-1:end) linearizes the time and page dimensions, producing [chan x freq x 2] instead of retaining [chan x freq x time]. Line 270 then calls limo_tfce(3, squeeze(Fval(:,:,:,1))) on this mangled array, so TFCE is silently computed over a corrupted 2-sample fake-time axis of wrong F-values, yielding an invalid observed statistic map (and invalid significance after comparison to the null).
minimal_fix: Make the ess trim dimension-aware: replace line 259 with `if strcmpi(LIMO.Analysis,'Time-Frequency'); Fval = Fval(:,:,:,end-1:end); else; Fval = Fval(:,:,end-1:end); end` so the 4-D TF ess keeps [chan x freq x time x 2] before the type-3 TFCE call.
verifier_reasoning: Real bug. For Time-Frequency the observed ess/contrast file is stored 4-D [chan x freq x time x pages] (limo_contrast.m:850 reshapes via limo_tf_4d_reshape before save; pages = mean,se,df,F,p = 5). The else-branch of limo_tfce_handling assumes this 4-D layout everywhere else (line 264/270 squeeze(Fval(:,:,:,1)); H0 side line 289/303 squeeze(H0_Fval(:,:,:,1,b)) on the 5-D H0_ess produced at limo_contrast.m:938). Line 259 alone uses a 3-subscript trim Fval(:,:,end-1:end). Under MATLAB semantics three subscripts on a 4-D array linearize dims 3 and 4, so end-1:end selects the last two entries of the flattened time*page axis, giving [chan x freq x 2] — freq/time collapsed and only the final time slice's F,p retained. The subsequent squeeze(Fval(:,:,:,1)) then feeds a [chan x freq x 2] array to limo_tfce type 3 (which expects [chan x freq x time]), computing TFCE on a corrupted 2-point map with no error thrown. Reachable via a 2nd-level rep-measures/ANOVA contrast on TF data with tfce enabled (filename ess_*.mat lands in the else-branch, contains('ess') true). Not a duplicate of the known 303/289 findings (those are wrong limo_tfce type arguments in the null branch) or 153 (R2 thmaps capture); this is a separate dimension-collapse in the observed ess trim. Severity medium: silent statistically-wrong observed TFCE map, but narrow trigger (Time-Frequency AND ess contrast AND TFCE).

summary: The observed-data clustering prefers bwlabeln with its default 8-connectivity while limo_tfcluster_make prefers spm_bwlabel with 6/4-connectivity, so on systems with both toolboxes the null and observed clusters use different neighbourhoods, invalidating FWER control.
detail: limo_tfcluster_make checks spm_bwlabel FIRST (lines 55-56/86-87) and uses spm_bwlabel(...,6), which on a 2D freq*time slice is 4-connectivity. limo_tfcluster_test checks bwlabel/bwlabeln FIRST (lines 57-58/89-90) and calls bwlabeln(...) with NO connectivity argument, defaulting to conndef(2,'maximal')=8-connectivity. On a machine where both the Image Processing Toolbox and SPM are installed, the H0 max-cluster distribution is built from 4-connected clusters while the observed clusters are 8-connected (merging diagonal neighbours). Larger observed clusters are then compared against a null built from smaller clusters, making the cluster test anti-conservative and the corrected p-values/threshold statistically inconsistent. limo_findcluster correctly passes explicit connectivity (spm 6 vs bwlabeln 4, both 4-conn) — tfcluster_test is the odd one out by omitting the bwlabeln connectivity argument and inverting the preference order.
failure_scenario: Machine has both the Image Processing Toolbox and a compiled spm_bwlabel mex (exist('spm_bwlabel','file')==3). Run limo_tfcluster_make on bootstrap data: it takes the spm_bwlabel(...,6) branch, so the H0 threshold th.max/th.elec is built from 4-connected (in-plane) clusters. Then run limo_tfcluster_test on an observed orif/orip where the significant points (orip<=alpha) form a diagonal chain in the freq*time plane. test takes the bwlabeln(...) branch (default 8-connectivity), merging those diagonally adjacent points into ONE large cluster with a large summed F. Because the null distribution never saw such merged clusters (its 4-conn clustering kept them separate, yielding smaller max sums), the observed cluster sum exceeds th.max(U) and is flagged significant at corrected p<alpha when the neighbourhood-consistent test would not flag it -- a false 'significant' correction, i.e. anti-conservative FWER.
minimal_fix: Make the toolbox-preference order and the connectivity identical in both make and test. Simplest: in limo_tfcluster_test replace the two clustering blocks so they mirror limo_tfcluster_make -- check spm_bwlabel first with connectivity 6 and, in the bwlabeln fallback, pass an explicit 4-connectivity: [L,NUM]=bwlabeln(squeeze(orip(e,:,:))<=alphav,conndef(2,'minimal')) (or bwlabeln(...,4)). Equivalently, fix make's bwlabeln fallback to also use 4-conn. The invariant to enforce: the connectivity used to cluster the observed data must equal the connectivity used to build the H0 distribution, regardless of which toolbox is present.
verifier_reasoning: Verified in source. limo_tfcluster_make (lines 55-58, 86-89) checks spm_bwlabel FIRST (exist==3) and calls spm_bwlabel(...,6), which on a 2D freq*time slice is 4-connectivity; only if spm is absent does it fall to bwlabeln with no connectivity arg (8-conn). limo_tfcluster_test (lines 57-60, 89-92) inverts this: it checks IPT FIRST (exist('bwlabel','file')~=0) and calls bwlabeln(...) with NO connectivity argument, defaulting to conndef(2,'maximal')=8-connectivity, only falling to spm_bwlabel(...,6) otherwise. On a system with both a compiled spm_bwlabel mex and the Image Processing Toolbox, make builds the H0 max-cluster distribution with 4-connected clustering while test clusters the observed F-map with 8-connected clustering. 8-connectivity merges diagonally adjacent supra-threshold points into a single larger cluster with a larger summed-F, which is then compared against a threshold derived from systematically smaller 4-connected null clusters. This makes the test anti-conservative and breaks FWER control. limo_findcluster elsewhere passes explicit connectivity consistently; tfcluster_test is the outlier by both inverting toolbox preference and omitting the bwlabeln connectivity argument. This is a real, reachable statistical-correctness defect. Severity medium: it requires both toolboxes present to manifest and degrades (rather than crashes) the correction, but it silently invalidates the multiple-comparisons control that is the whole purpose of the function. Not a duplicate of the known line-69 finding, which is about the per-cluster overwrite of the entire (e,:,:) pvalue/mask map inside the cluster loop -- a distinct root cause.

summary: The programmatic path never cd's to the LIMO file's folder, so `load Yr` / `load Betas` load from the caller's cwd rather than beside the LIMO file.
detail: The GUI branch does `cd(dir)` (line 20) so the hard-coded `load Yr` (line 44) and `load Betas` (line 61) resolve relative to the LIMO folder. The else/programmatic branch loads `varargin{1}` (which may be a full path) but never changes directory. Consequently `load Yr` and `load Betas` are resolved against the caller's current working directory, which generally is NOT where Yr.mat/Betas.mat live, causing a load failure or loading stale data from an unrelated directory. Like the X defect, this is presently masked by the line-36 tautology but is a separate latent bug.
failure_scenario: Precondition: the line-36 tautology must first be fixed (e.g. `||`→`&&`) so the programmatic branch can proceed past line 37. Then, from a cwd that is not the LIMO folder, call `limo_create_files('/path/to/study/sub-01/LIMO.mat',1,'raw')`. Line 33 loads the LIMO struct by absolute path (fine), but line 44's `load Yr` looks for `Yr.mat` in the caller's cwd, not `/path/to/study/sub-01/`. Result: either MATLAB errors ('Unable to read file 'Yr'. No such file or directory.') or, if an unrelated `Yr.mat` happens to exist in cwd, it silently loads the wrong data cube and writes corrupted `raw_data_regressor_*.mat` files. The same failure occurs for `type='modeled'` at line 61's `load Betas`. (Note: the programmatic 'raw' path also references an undefined `X` at line 49, since X is only assigned in the GUI branch — a separate defect.)
minimal_fix: In the programmatic branch, resolve Yr/Betas relative to the LIMO file's folder rather than cwd. Either add `cd(fileparts(varargin{1}))` (or push/pop the directory) in the else branch mirroring the GUI branch, or load by absolute path: `p = fileparts(varargin{1}); load(fullfile(p,'Yr.mat'))` and `load(fullfile(p,'Betas.mat'))`. Prefer the absolute-path form to avoid mutating the caller's working directory.
verifier_reasoning: The code genuinely differs between the two branches: the GUI branch (line 20) does `cd(dir)` before the relative `load LIMO.mat`, so the later `load Yr` (44) and `load Betas` (61) resolve against the LIMO folder; the programmatic branch (line 33) does `load(varargin{1})` with a full path but never changes directory, so those relative loads resolve against the caller's cwd. That is a real defect. However, it is currently UNREACHABLE via the programmatic path: line 36's tautology (`~strcmp(type,'raw') || ~strcmp(type,'modeled')`, true for every input) forces an `error` at line 37 before execution ever reaches line 44/61. So no concrete input reaches line 44 through the programmatic branch today — it only manifests after the sibling line-36 bug is corrected. Hence PLAUSIBLE (real latent bug, contingent on fixing line 36), not CONFIRMED. It is NOT a duplicate of the line-36 finding: that finding is the boolean-tautology root cause on line 36; this is the missing-cd/relative-load root cause on lines 44 and 61 — different lines, different fix. Severity medium: once unmasked, the relative `load Yr`/`load Betas` will either crash with 'Unable to read file Yr' or silently load a stale/unrelated Yr.mat or Betas.mat from cwd and write corrupted regressor output files, which is a silent data-corruption risk; but it is gated behind (a) the line-36 fix and (b) cwd differing from the LIMO folder.

summary: TF branch builds `subject_name` from all of `current_subject` rather than `current_subject(1)` as the Time and Frequency branches do, producing a multi-element cell that breaks the subsequent cellfun/strcmp when more than one dataset matches the data dir.
detail: Line 270: `subject_name = {STUDY.datasetinfo(current_subject).subject};`. The Time (line 69) and Frequency (line 167) branches use `current_subject(1)` to guarantee a single subject name. If `current_subject` (from the `find` at line 269) returns more than one index, `subject_name` becomes a 1xM cell, and line 274 `repmat(subject_name,n,1)` then produces an n x M cell that mismatches the n x 1 `Cluster_matrix.clust(c).subj'` in the cellfun(@strcmp,...) call, causing a size error.
failure_scenario: TF analysis, LIMO.Type='Components', LIMO.data.cluster~=0, with an EEGLAB STUDY where two or more STUDY.datasetinfo entries have filepaths that resolve (via rel2fullpath) to the same directory as LIMO.data.data_dir (e.g. a subject with multiple session/condition .set files in one folder). Then current_subject = find(...) has length >=2, subject_name = {STUDY.datasetinfo(current_subject).subject} is a 1x2+ cell, and line 274 cellfun(@strcmp, Cluster_matrix.clust(c).subj', repmat(subject_name,n,1)) throws a matrix-dimension mismatch (n x 1 vs n x M), aborting the clustering reorder. With a single dataset per directory current_subject is scalar and no error occurs.
minimal_fix: Match the Time/Frequency branches: change line 270 to `subject_name = {STUDY.datasetinfo(current_subject(1)).subject};`.
verifier_reasoning: Real, reachable inconsistency. Line 270 in the Time-Frequency component-clustering branch builds subject_name from the full index vector current_subject, whereas the analogous Time (line 69) and Frequency (line 167) branches take current_subject(1). current_subject = find(...) over dsetinfo returns one index per datasetinfo entry whose resolved filepath equals data_dir. In EEGLAB STUDYs a subject can have several datasets stored in the same directory (multi-session/condition), so find can return >1 index. When it does, subject_name becomes a 1xM cell; at line 274 repmat(subject_name,n,1) is n x M while Cluster_matrix.clust(c).subj' is n x 1, so cellfun(@strcmp,...) errors on size mismatch and TF component clustering aborts. The single-dataset-per-dir case works fine, so it is a conditional edge-case crash. Distinct root cause from known findings: line 271 (newY preallocation/dimensionality), line 150 (component count), line 289 (unconditional struct2mat), line 255 (double-squaring) — none share this line or cause, so not a duplicate. Severity low: requires the specific but realistic multi-dataset-in-one-folder configuration, only affects the niche TF+Components+clustering path, and manifests as a clean error rather than silent corruption.

summary: The multivariate bootstrap loop iterates `for e = 1:size(Y,1)` but indexes `channel = array(e)`, where array (line 529) only holds the non-NaN channels; when any channel is empty, array is shorter than size(Y,1) and array(e) indexes out of bounds.
detail: array = find(~isnan(Y(:,1,1))) at line 529 excludes all-NaN channels. The mass-univariate loop correctly uses `for e = 1:length(array)` (line 535), but the multivariate loop at line 679 uses `for e = 1:size(Y,1)` while still doing channel = array(e) at line 680. If length(array) < size(Y,1) (any empty channel), array(e) throws 'Index exceeds the number of array elements' once e passes length(array).
failure_scenario: A multivariate (type 2) contrast bootstrap with a single-column design (p=1, e.g. a one-sample/constant multivariate design so the C0 computation at lines 709/712 does not itself crash) AND at least one all-NaN/empty channel so that length(array) < size(Y,1). The loop `for e = 1:size(Y,1)` reaches e = length(array)+1 and `channel = array(e)` throws "Index exceeds the number of array elements", aborting the bootstrap. For the more common multi-predictor multivariate designs, an unrelated dimension-mismatch at line 712 (`eye(rank(X)) - c*pinv(c)`, rank(X)=1 due to the linear-indexing bug at 686-687) crashes the first iteration before the out-of-bounds is ever reached.
minimal_fix: Change line 679 from `for e = 1:size(Y,1)` to `for e = 1:length(array)`, matching the mass-univariate loop at line 535 so only valid (non-empty) channels are iterated.
verifier_reasoning: Real defect: line 679 loops `for e = 1:size(Y,1)` but line 680 does `channel = array(e)`, where `array = find(~isnan(Y(:,1,1)))` (line 529) holds only non-empty channels. In the multivariate branch `Y` is never reassigned (the mass-univariate block at 532-668 is mutually exclusive), so `size(Y,1) = n_channels`. With any all-NaN channel, `length(array) < size(Y,1)` and `array(e)` overruns once e passes length(array). The intent is clear from the parallel mass-univariate loop (line 535) which correctly uses `for e = 1:length(array)` with the same "skip empty channels" array. summary: The GUI branch ignores the user-selected file and hard-codes `load LIMO.mat`, so a differently named selection loads the wrong file or errors.
detail: uigetfile at line 19 returns the chosen filename in `file` and its folder in `dir`. Line 20 then does `cd(dir); load LIMO.mat;` which literally loads a file named 'LIMO.mat' from the directory, completely disregarding the `file` variable the user picked. If the user selects any .mat file not named exactly 'LIMO.mat' (the dialog filter permits navigating/selecting other names), the code either loads a stale LIMO.mat that happens to be in that folder or throws 'Unable to read file LIMO.mat'. The `file` output is never used.
failure_scenario: In the GUI (no-arg) path the user opens the dialog, browses to a folder, and selects a .mat file whose name is not exactly 'LIMO.mat' (e.g. subj03_LIMO.mat) — either by clicking it (the *.mat extension filter shows all .mat files) or by typing the name. Line 20 ignores `file` and runs `load LIMO.mat`. If that folder also holds an unrelated LIMO.mat, its struct loads silently, giving a wrong LIMO.design.X and producing incorrect regressor files with no error; if no LIMO.mat exists there, `load LIMO.mat` throws 'Unable to read file LIMO.mat'.
minimal_fix: Use the returned selection instead of the hardcoded name: replace `cd (dir); load LIMO.mat;` with `cd(dir); load(fullfile(dir,file));` (optionally add a cancel guard `if isequal(file,0), return; end` before it since uigetfile returns 0 on cancel).
verifier_reasoning: Line 19 captures the user's chosen filename in `file`, but line 20 (`cd(dir); load LIMO.mat; X = LIMO.design.X;`) hardcodes the literal `load LIMO.mat` and never references `file`. Because `uigetfile('LIMO.mat',...)` uses the filename only as a default while filtering by extension (*.mat), the user can click or type any other .mat name; `file` is then silently discarded. Root cause is the dead `file` variable + hardcoded load at line 20 — distinct from the known line-36 type-validation tautology (different line and mechanism), so not a duplicate. Reachable at least via typing a name, and in practice via clicking any *.mat in the browsed folder. Severity held to low because the standard workflow (selecting LIMO.mat itself) works and triggering the fault requires an atypical non-default selection; the report's 'medium' overstates likelihood.

summary: In the programmatic (varargin) calling path, X is never defined, so the raw branch (line 49) and modeled branch (line 63) reference an undefined X.

Later, raw processing at line 49 uses `squeeze(X(:,i))` and modeled processing at line 63 uses `size(X,2)` — both would error 'Undefined variable X' when the function is invoked programmatically as documented (`limo_creates_files(File,parameters,type)`). Likewise limo_create_files('LIMO.mat',1,'modeled') errors at line 63 size(X,2). In the current unpatched code the crash instead occurs earlier at line 36, so the undefined-X fault is masked but real.
minimal_fix: In the else branch after load(varargin{1}) (around line 33), add X = LIMO.design.X; mirroring the GUI branch at line 20. (Line 36's tautology should also be fixed separately, e.g. to ~(strcmp(type,'raw') || strcmp(type,'modeled')).)
verifier_reasoning: Code confirms the claim: the else/programmatic branch (lines 32-39) does load(varargin{1}) which only brings the LIMO struct into scope and never assigns X, yet the raw branch (line 49, squeeze(X(:,i))) and modeled branch (line 63, size(X,2)) both reference X. The GUI branch assigns X = LIMO.design.X at line 20, so the GUI path is fine; only the programmatic path lacks the assignment. This is a distinct root cause from the already-known line-36 tautology (undefined variable vs malformed strcmp boolean), on different lines. It is NOT a duplicate: fixing line 36 alone still leaves the function crashing on undefined X, so this requires its own fix. However, it is currently latent/unreachable because the line-36 tautology throws for every type value before execution ever reaches line 43+. load would only define X if LIMO.mat contained a bare X variable, which it does not. summary: The TF NaN-trial removal marks a trial for removal if ANY categorical column is NaN, whereas the non-TF limo_design_matrix removes a trial only when ALL columns are NaN, silently dropping trials in multi-factor TF designs.
detail: Line 158: `check = find(sum(isnan(Cat),2));` flags any row whose columns sum to a nonzero NaN count, i.e. any row with at least one NaN. The equivalent line in limo_design_matrix.m (line 129) is `check = find(sum(isnan(Cat),2)==size(Cat,2));`, which flags only rows where every column is NaN (the documented 'mark a trial for removal' convention). For a multi-column (N-way factorial) Cat where a single factor column is NaN on a valid trial, the TF path removes that trial from both Cat and Y while the standard path keeps it, producing a different (smaller) design than the time/frequency pipeline for identical inputs.
failure_scenario: TF first-level analysis with a 2+ factor categorical design, e.g. Cat = [1 1; 2 NaN; 1 2] and Y sized [chan x freq x time x 3]. limo_design_matrix_tf.m:158 computes find(sum(isnan(Cat),2)) = find([0;1;0]) = 2, so trial 2 is stripped from Cat/Cont/Y, fitting the model on 2 trials. The equivalent time/frequency call to limo_design_matrix.m:129 computes find(sum(isnan(Cat),2)==2) = find([0;0;0]) = [], retaining all 3 trials. Same inputs, different N and different fitted design between the two pipelines.
minimal_fix: Change line 158 to match limo_design_matrix.m:129: `check = find(sum(isnan(Cat),2)==size(Cat,2));` so only fully-NaN rows are trimmed, making the TF and time/frequency pipelines consistent with the documented all-NaN removal convention.
verifier_reasoning: The code difference is real and verified directly. limo_design_matrix.m:129 flags a trial for NaN-removal only when EVERY categorical column is NaN (`find(sum(isnan(Cat),2)==size(Cat,2))`), the documented "mark a trial for removal" convention. limo_design_matrix_tf.m:158 (`find(sum(isnan(Cat),2))`) flags any row with at least one NaN. For single-column Cat the two are identical, so the divergence only manifests with multi-column (N-way factorial) Cat where a trial has a NaN in one factor column but valid values in the others. In that case the TF pipeline silently drops the trial (from Cat, Cont, and Y) while the time/frequency pipeline retains it, producing a different N and a different fitted GLM for identical inputs. This is a concrete, reproducible cross-pipeline inconsistency and a departure from the documented all-NaN removal convention, so it is a genuine defect. Severity is low because it requires the uncommon case of a partial-NaN row in a multi-factor categorical design (a user following the all-NaN convention to mark removals is unaffected, and a fully-NaN row is handled identically by both). Not a duplicate: the only known finding in this file is the undefined `basic_design` at line 303, a completely different root cause.

summary: The first (shuffled) observation is never placed in any test fold, so k-fold CV never evaluates prediction error on all data.
detail: foldindex = 1:Nfold:N with foldindex(1)=1, and testindex for each fold is `foldindex(fold)+1:foldindex(fold+1)` (line 63). The union of test indices over folds 1..k is therefore foldindex(1)+1 : foldindex(k+1) = 2:N. Observation 1 is excluded from every test set and always sits in the training set, biasing the cross-validation slightly and making it not a true partition of the data. The fold boundaries should start at 0 (e.g. foldindex = 0:Nfold:N) so the first fold's test set is 1:foldindex(2).
failure_scenario: Call limo_prederror(LIMO) with the default k=5 on data with N=10 subjects. Nfold=2, foldindex becomes [1 3 5 7 9 10]. The five folds' test index ranges are 2:3, 4:5, 6:7, 8:9, 10:10, whose union is 2:10. Shuffled observation index 1 is placed in the training set of all five folds and is never scored as test data, so its residual/bias/variance are excluded from the averaged errors and the CV is not a valid partition of the data.
minimal_fix: Start the fold boundaries at 0 so the first fold's test set includes index 1: replace foldindex = 1:Nfold:N; with foldindex = 0:Nfold:N; foldindex(k+1) = N; leaving testindex = foldindex(fold)+1:foldindex(fold+1) unchanged so fold 1 tests 1:Nfold.
verifier_reasoning: At line 56 foldindex = 1:Nfold:N and line 63 testindex = foldindex(fold)+1:foldindex(fold+1). Because every fold's test set starts at foldindex(fold)+1, the union of test indices over folds 1..k is foldindex(1)+1:foldindex(k+1) = 2:N. Index 1 always falls in trainindex = setdiff(1:N,testindex) and is never passed to geterrors as test data, so its prediction error/bias/variance never contribute to the averaged output. This is a true off-by-one: the fold partition is not a partition. Verified concretely with N=10,k=5 (test sets 2:3,4:5,6:7,8:9,10:10). The bug is reachable through the ordinary default call limo_prederror(LIMO) (nargin==1), which defines N at line 42 and reaches line 63 normally, so it is independent of the known line-45 defect. Not a duplicate: the known finding is a different root cause (N undefined when nargin==2) at a different line (45). Severity is low because it only silently drops one shuffled observation from the test evaluation (statistical bias in a diagnostic routine), not a crash or major corruption, and the relative effect diminishes as N grows.

summary: The Components dimension-mismatch handler references an undefined variable `expected_chanlocs` and compares an MException to a string, so the diagnostic path itself errors and never prints.
detail: Inside getdata's catch (lines 1998-2005): line 1999 `if strcmp(dim_error,'Subscripted assignment dimension mismatch.')` compares the MException object dim_error to a char (always false, so the message never shows); it should be `dim_error.message` or `dim_error.identifier`. Line 2001 `if isempty(expected_chanlocs)` references a variable that does not exist in getdata's scope (its args are stattest,analysis_type,first_frame,last_frame,subj_chanlocs,LIMO), so reaching it throws 'Undefined function or variable expected_chanlocs', masking the real component-size error.
failure_scenario: Component (ICA) group analysis, Full scalp analysis, LIMO.Type='Components', where subjects have different numbers of ICs. The assignment data(:,:,:,index)=tmp(...) at line 1994/1996 throws 'Subscripted assignment dimension mismatch'. Control enters the catch at line 1998. Line 1999 strcmp(dim_error, '...') is false (MException vs char), so the guidance messages at 2000-2002 are skipped entirely and line 2001's undefined-variable reference is never evaluated. The error is silently swallowed: nothing prints, and the loop continues to index=index+1 and removed(i)=0, so the user gets no indication why a subject's data is missing/misaligned. The reported secondary crash on expected_chanlocs is only hypothetical (would occur only if the strcmp were fixed to be true without also fixing line 2001).
minimal_fix: Replace line 1999 with strcmp(dim_error.message,'Subscripted assignment dimension mismatch.') (or check dim_error.identifier), and replace expected_chanlocs on line 2001 with LIMO.data.expected_chanlocs. Ideally also rethrow or record the subject as removed so a real IC-size mismatch is not silently ignored.
verifier_reasoning: Verified at limo_random_select.m:1998-2005. (1) Line 1999 strcmp(dim_error,'...') compares the caught MException object dim_error to a char; MATLAB strcmp returns false (0) for a non-char object rather than erroring, so the intended message 'you are trying to match matrices of ICs of different size' never prints. This is a real defect. (2) Line 2001 references expected_chanlocs, which is genuinely undefined in getdata's scope (args are stattest,analysis_type,first_frame,last_frame,subj_chanlocs,LIMO; the real field is LIMO.data.expected_chanlocs). Both defects exist exactly as reported. Severity is low: because the always-false strcmp guards the block, line 2001 is never actually reached, so no secondary crash occurs in practice; the net observable effect is that a genuine IC-size mismatch is silently swallowed by the catch with no diagnostic printed, and the loop still does index=index+1 / removed(i)=0 as if the subject were stored (a latent misalignment concern, but the immediate reported issue is the dead diagnostic path). Not a duplicate of any listed finding — the known list covers line 1956 (backward loop) and others, but not the 1999-2001 catch block.

summary: Input dimension check uses && instead of ||, so a malformed input where only one array is non-3D is not rejected.
detail: Line 31 `if ndims(Y1r)~=3 && ndims(Y2r) ~=3` errors only when BOTH inputs fail the 3D requirement. If one input is a valid 3D matrix and the other is 2D (or otherwise not 3D), the condition is false and no error is raised; execution continues into the channel loop where `squeeze(Y1r(channel,:,:))` / `squeeze(Y2r(channel,:,:))` produce mismatched shapes and the GLM concatenation `[Z1 Z2]'` or covariance step misbehaves or crashes with an opaque error. The validating intent (both must be 3D) requires ||.
failure_scenario: Call limo_BrownForsythe(Y1r, Y2r) with Y1r a 3D matrix of size 3x4x5 (ndims 3, 60 elements) and Y2r a 2D matrix of size 12x5 (ndims 2, 60 elements). The 4D reshape branch (line 25) is skipped since not both are 4D. Line 31 evaluates 3~=3 (false) && 2~=3 (true) = false, so the dimension guard does not fire. The numel check at line 35 passes (60 == 60). In the loop, Z1 = squeeze(Y1r(1,:,:)) is 4x5 while Z2 = squeeze(Y2r(1,:,:)) is 1x5, so line 52 [Z1 Z2] throws 'Dimensions of arrays being concatenated are not consistent' instead of the intended clean 'data must be 4D/3D matrices' error.
minimal_fix: Change && to || on line 31: `if ndims(Y1r)~=3 || ndims(Y2r) ~=3`. This preserves the valid 3D/3D case (both false -> no error) while correctly rejecting any input where either array is not 3D.
verifier_reasoning: Line 31 `if ndims(Y1r)~=3 && ndims(Y2r) ~=3` fires only when BOTH inputs fail the 3D requirement, but the validating intent is that BOTH must be 3D, which requires ||. A malformed call with one 3D and one non-3D array of matching numel slips past this guard and past the numel check at line 35, then crashes opaquely inside the loop. This is a genuine defect distinct from the known line-43 finding (loop counter misused as channel index), which is a different line and root cause, so not a duplicate.

summary: The 'parameter not computed' warnings have format-string / argument mismatches and use the wrong variable, producing garbled messages.
detail: Line 412: `warning('subject %g, parameter %g not computed: \n the design only includes %g regressors plus the constant', Paths{i}, size(LIMO.design.X,2))` has three %g conversions but only two arguments, and Paths{i} (a char path) is fed to a %g numeric conversion (printed as character codes). The parameter value is never printed. The mirrored warnings at lines 781 and 783 additionally use the loop counter `j` instead of `parameters(j)` (line 780/783), so they report the wrong parameter index. These are message-only defects (no wrong result) but emit misleading/incorrect diagnostics.
failure_scenario: User calls limo_central_tendency_and_ci selecting a continuous regressor (or a parameter index greater than size(LIMO.design.X,2)) for a subject whose design does not include it. Control reaches line 411/412 (single-parameter path) or 780/781 (Evaluate single conditions loop). The warning that fires prints the subject path as a string of numeric character codes, repeats the whole sentence multiple times, mislabels the regressor count, and omits the actual parameter number; in the loop branch it also reports the loop counter j instead of the true parameter parameters(j). The computation itself is unaffected — only the diagnostic is garbled and misleading about which subject/parameter was skipped.
minimal_fix:
In lines 412, 414, 781, 783: use %s for the char path and pass the correct values, matching conversion count to argument count. E.g. line 412: warning('subject %s, parameter %g not computed: 
 the design only includes %g regressors plus the constant', Paths{i}, max(parameters), size(LIMO.design.X,2)); line 414: warning('subject %s, 
 parameter %g not computed - continuous regressor', Paths{i}, max(parameters)); line 781: warning('subject %s, parameter %g not computed: 
 the design only includes %g regressors plus the constant', Paths{i}, parameters(j), size(LIMO.design.X,2)); line 783: warning('subject %s, 
 parameter %g not computed - continuous regressor', Paths{i}, parameters(j)); and change line 780's test max(j) to parameters(j).
verifier_reasoning: Verified against the source. Lines 411-415 are reached when the guard at 332-334 fails (an out-of-range or continuous regressor is selected), and lines 780-784 in the 'Evaluate single conditions' loop (for j=length(parameters):-1:1, real param = parameters(j)) are reached analogously. Claims are all accurate: (a) lines 412 and 781 have three %g conversions but only two arguments; (b) Paths{i} is a char path passed to %g, so MATLAB expands each character to its numeric code and, because it recycles the format string cyclically over the flattened value stream, prints a garbled, repeated message with size(LIMO.design.X,2) mislabeled and the parameter number never passed at all; (c) line 414 similarly feeds the char path to %g; (d) line 783 prints the loop index j instead of parameters(j), and line 780 tests max(j) rather than parameters(j), so the wrong parameter index is reported. However this is strictly a diagnostic-message defect: no statistical result is altered, nothing crashes (sprintf/warning tolerate the type/arg mismatch), and no data is corrupted. Hence low severity. Not a duplicate of the three known findings (360 zero-padding estimator, 789 non-scalar size() guard, 1069 missing waitbar init) — distinct lines and root cause.

summary: When boot_maxclustersum is empty (the documented 4-argument call), the elec-branch p-value computation divides by length([])==0, yielding NaN p-values for significant clusters instead of a real p-value.
detail: boot_maxclustersum defaults to [] at lines 45-47 when the function is called with 4 args (as done in limo_mstat_values.m:313 and :321). In the elec branch, for a significant cluster line 108 computes p = 1 - sum(maxval(C) >= boot_maxclustersum)./length(boot_maxclustersum). With boot_maxclustersum==[], sum(scalar>=[]) is 0 and length([]) is 0, so 0/0 = NaN and p = NaN. The guard `if p==0` at line 109 is false for NaN, so pval(channel,L==C) is set to NaN. The returned per-cluster p-values are therefore NaN whenever the null distribution was not supplied, rather than a defined value or an error. Current callers that omit boot_maxclustersum happen to discard pval, which is why this is low severity, but any caller that requests pval on the 4-arg path receives silent NaNs.
failure_scenario: Call the documented 4-argument form and request the documented pval output: th = limo_ecluster_make(bootf,bootp,0.05) for a single electrode (2D input), then [~,pval] = limo_ecluster_test(orif,orip,th,0.05) where orif contains a temporal cluster whose F-sum exceeds th.elec. boot_maxclustersum defaults to [] (lines 45-47). The elec branch executes (th.elec always present), the cluster is flagged significant, and line 108 computes p = 1 - sum(scalar>=[])./length([]) = 1 - 0/0 = NaN; the p==0 guard does not catch NaN, so the returned pval entries for that cluster are NaN rather than a valid corrected p-value. In-repo callers currently discard pval, limiting real-world impact.
minimal_fix: Guard the p-value computation on ~isempty(boot_maxclustersum). When boot_maxclustersum is empty, either skip populating pval (leave the NaN-preallocated value or set to NaN by design) or, better, require boot_maxclustersum whenever the pval output is requested (e.g. if nargout>1 && isempty(boot_maxclustersum), error/warn). Concretely, wrap lines 108-112 in if ~isempty(boot_maxclustersum) so the divide-by-zero path is never taken.
verifier_reasoning: The elec branch runs unconditionally because limo_ecluster_make always sets th.elec. With boot_maxclustersum=[] (the nargin<5 default, i.e. the documented 4-arg call), line 90's guard size([],1)>1 is false so no collapse occurs, and line 108 evaluates 1 - sum(maxval(C) >= [])./length([]) = 1 - 0/0 = NaN for a significant cluster. Line 109's if p==0 is false for NaN so the 1/length fallback is skipped, and line 112 stores NaN into pval. Both pval and maxval are documented outputs and the 4-arg signature is documented (header line 2), so [~,pval]=limo_ecluster_test(M,P,th,0.05) with a significant temporal cluster is a legitimate call that returns NaN instead of a valid corrected p-value. Severity is low because the only two in-repo 4-arg callers (limo_mstat_values.m:313 and :321) request only sigcluster and discard pval, so no shipped analysis path is corrupted; the 5-arg caller (limo_clustering.m:104) supplies boot_maxclustersum. NOT a duplicate of the line-91 finding: that bug requires a non-empty multi-row boot_maxclustersum being collapsed to a scalar by max(...,[],1); this one requires an empty boot_maxclustersum causing 0/0. The trigger conditions are mutually exclusive and the anchored lines/root causes differ (108 empty-division vs 91 dimension-collapse).

summary: limo_error() is called but no such function exists in the toolbox, so the intended 'no neighbouring matrix' error path throws an 'Undefined function' error instead.
detail: Line 796 calls limo_error('no neighbouring matrix returned, ...') when limo_expected_chanlocs returns an empty neighbouring matrix. There is no limo_error.m anywhere in the toolbox (only limo_errordlg.m, limo_warndlg.m, limo_questdlg.m exist), and no local/nested definition; grep for 'function ... limo_error' finds none. The same non-existent limo_error is also referenced in limo_central_tendency_and_ci.m, limo_display_results.m and limo_random_select.m, confirming it is genuinely missing rather than a local helper. When reached, MATLAB raises 'Undefined function or variable limo_error' rather than the intended descriptive error, and the descriptive message is lost.
failure_scenario: User requests TFCE correction for a GLM analysis where no channeighbstructmat is pre-supplied, so the else branch (line 794) calls limo_expected_chanlocs. If that returns an empty neighbouring_matrix (e.g. chanlocs cannot yield a neighbour structure), execution hits line 796 and MATLAB errors with 'Undefined function or variable limo_error' instead of the intended message 'no neighbouring matrix returned, try creating with limo tools'. The run still aborts, but with an opaque error masking the real cause.
minimal_fix: Replace limo_error(...) at line 796 with the builtin error(...), matching the sibling branch at line 787: error('no neighbouring matrix returned, try creating with limo tools'). (Optionally apply the same substitution to the other three files referencing limo_error, or add a limo_error.m wrapper.)
verifier_reasoning: limo_glm_handling.m:796 calls limo_error('no neighbouring matrix returned, try creating with limo tools') inside the TFCE-with-computed-neighbours branch. No limo_error.m exists in the toolbox (only limo_errordlg/warndlg/questdlg), and grep for a function/nested/local definition of limo_error yields nothing anywhere on the path; the same undefined name is also referenced in limo_random_select.m, limo_central_tendency_and_ci.m, and limo_display_results.m. The sibling branch at line 787 correctly uses builtin error(). So when limo_expected_chanlocs returns an empty neighbouring_matrix, MATLAB throws 'Undefined function limo_error' instead of the intended descriptive error, and the descriptive message is lost. This is a real, reachable defect. Severity is low: the intended behavior at this point is already to abort the run with an error, so the practical harm is a confusing/opaque error message rather than a clean one — no silent corruption or wrong statistical result; execution halts either way. Not a duplicate: the three known findings in this file are at lines 717 (covariate H0 filename desc- bug), 820 (TFCE interaction loop bound), and 449 (H0 preallocation sizing) — different lines and unrelated root causes.

summary: Continuous-covariate loop indexes Eigen_values_continuous by covariate number (:,n), but each iteration holds only one decomposition
detail: Inside the per-covariate loop (827-856), `Eigen_values_continuous` is recomputed each iteration for covariate n's rank-1 hypothesis (line 835). The code then reads `Eigen_values_continuous(:,n)` at lines 847, 848 and 852, i.e. it indexes the n-th COLUMN as though the variable held one column per covariate. It never does: it is a single decomposition. Currently this is masked only because limo_decomp's first (mis-captured) output is a p-by-p eigenvector matrix, so column n happens to exist for n<=p — but then max(...) is taken over an eigenvector component, which is statistically meaningless. If the eigenvalue-capture bug were fixed to return the p-by-1 eigenvalue vector, `(:,n)` would throw an index-out-of-bounds error for the 2nd and later covariates.
failure_scenario: Call limo_mglm with a multivariate response Y (size(Y,2)>=2) and a design containing two or more continuous covariates (nb_continuous>=2), reaching the else branch at line 822. Iteration n=2 evaluates Eigen_values_continuous(:,2). Current behavior: Eigen_values_continuous holds the size(Y,2) x size(Y,2) eigenvector matrix (bug #182), so (:,2) reads the 2nd eigenvector column and max() of it yields a nonsensical theta_continuous(2)/F_continuous_Roy(2)/V_continuous(2) — wrong Roy and Pillai statistics for the 2nd (and later) covariates. If bug #182 is later fixed so limo_decomp's single output returns a p x 1 eigenvalue vector, then Eigen_values_continuous(:,2) throws an index-out-of-bounds error, crashing every multivariate MANCOVA that has 2+ continuous covariates.
minimal_fix: At lines 847, 848 and 852 replace Eigen_values_continuous(:,n) with the current iteration's decomposition — either Eigen_values_continuous (the full vector, cleanest once #182 is fixed) or Eigen_values_continuous(:,1) — since the variable is recomputed each iteration and never accumulates one column per covariate.
verifier_reasoning: Real, distinct bug. In the multivariate continuous-covariate loop (827-856), `Eigen_values_continuous` is recomputed each iteration for covariate n (line 835), so it holds exactly one decomposition — never one column per covariate. Yet lines 847, 848, 852 read `Eigen_values_continuous(:,n)`, indexing by covariate number. Because `limo_decomp(E,H)` with a single output returns eigen_vectors (a size(Y,2) x size(Y,2) matrix; the E/H SSCP matrices are size(Y,2) square), column n exists whenever n <= size(Y,2), so for n=2 the code silently reads the 2nd eigenvector column and takes max() over eigenvector components — a meaningless theta/F/Pillai. This is a separate root cause from known bug #182 (which is about capturing eigenvectors vs eigenvalues): even if #182 is fixed so the variable becomes a p x 1 eigenvalue vector, `(:,n)` for n>=2 becomes an out-of-bounds index error. No known finding touches line 847 or the (:,n) indexing (line 841 is the same loop but a different dfe defect). Reachable: any multivariate MANCOVA design with >=2 continuous covariates (nb_continuous>=2) entering the else branch at line 822 runs the loop with n=2. Severity low: the entire multivariate branch is already corrupted by the critical #182, so this bug is currently masked (garbage on top of garbage) and only manifests as a hard crash once #182 is corrected — real but contingent and confined to MANCOVA-with-multiple-covariates.

summary: bootstrap_Callback checks isempty(handles.b) after str2double, but str2double never returns empty (it returns NaN), so bad/blank input propagates NaN as the bootstrap count.
detail: Line 138 sets handles.b = str2double(get(hObject,'String')). str2double returns a scalar NaN for an empty or non-numeric string, never []. Thus `if isempty(handles.b)` at line 139 is always false and can never reset handles.b to 0. The elseif `handles.b == 0` is also false for NaN (NaN==0 is false), and the guard at line 146 `if handles.b > 0 && handles.b < 1000` is false for NaN, so a blank/invalid entry leaves handles.b = NaN, which is then passed as 'nboot' to limo_random_select.
failure_scenario: User blanks the bootstrap text field or types a non-numeric string. bootstrap_Callback runs: line 138 sets handles.b = str2double(...) = NaN. isempty(NaN) is false (line 139), NaN==0 is false (line 141), and NaN>0 && NaN<1000 is false (line 146, which also means the line-148 num2bouble crash is NOT triggered on this path). handles.b = NaN is committed via guidata (line 154). When the user then launches a test (e.g. one sample t-test), limo_random_select is called with 'nboot',NaN instead of the intended 0/no-bootstrap, producing NaN-driven bootstrap behavior downstream.
minimal_fix: Replace the emptiness check with a NaN check: change line 139 `if isempty(handles.b)` to `if isnan(handles.b)`, so blank/invalid input resets handles.b = 0.
verifier_reasoning: Verified against limo_random_effect.m lines 136-154. str2double in MATLAB returns scalar NaN (never []) for empty or non-numeric strings, so isempty(handles.b) at line 139 is always false and the intended reset-to-0 never fires. NaN==0 (line 141) is false, and NaN>0 && NaN<1000 (line 146) is false, so handles.b remains NaN and is stored via guidata at line 154. It is then passed as 'nboot',handles.b to limo_random_select at lines 206/209/236/239/265/268/294/298/338/341. The reported detail is accurate on all MATLAB semantics. NOT a duplicate of the line-148 num2bouble finding: that crash is only reachable when 0<handles.b<1000 (line 146 true), whereas the NaN input path makes line 146 false and bypasses line 148 entirely — different line, different root cause, mutually exclusive branch. Severity low: it is a GUI input-validation edge case requiring the user to blank the field or type a non-number, and the downstream effect (NaN nboot) does not corrupt normal-usage results.

summary: The 'already computed, skip recompute' guard tests a filename pattern that never matches the names the code actually writes, so the skip branch is dead and the analysis always recomputes and overwrites existing results.
detail: Line 1052 guards the whole rep-measures computation with `if isempty(dir('Rep_ANOVA_Factor*.mat'))`. But the files this branch actually saves are named 'Rep_ANOVA_Main_effect_%g...' and 'Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_Factors_%s...' (lines 1158-1165), never 'Rep_ANOVA_Factor*'. Therefore `dir('Rep_ANOVA_Factor*.mat')` is always empty and the guard is always true, so the intended 'skip if results already exist' behavior is silently disabled and every invocation recomputes and overwrites the previous results. (Note: because the guard never skips, `Rep_filenames`/`IRep_filenames` are always defined before the later bootstrap/TFCE loops, so it doesn't crash — but the intended skip semantics are dead.)
failure_scenario: Run limo_random_robust with type 6 (repeated-measures ANOVA) in a directory that already contains completed results from a prior run (e.g. Rep_ANOVA_Main_effect_1.mat, Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_Factors_12.mat). Because `dir('Rep_ANOVA_Factor*.mat')` matches none of these actual filenames, `isempty(...)` returns true and the code re-enters the full computation block (lines 1053-1208), recomputing every channel and re-saving over the existing Rep_ANOVA_Main_effect_*.mat / Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_*.mat files (line 1175/1200/1206), destroying the previous outputs. The intended behavior — detect existing results and skip straight to the bootstrap section — never happens. No error is raised; the harm is wasted computation and silent overwrite of prior (possibly hand-inspected) results.
minimal_fix: Change the line 1052 guard to key off the filenames the code actually writes, e.g. `if isempty(dir('Rep_ANOVA_Main_effect*.mat')) && isempty(dir('Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_Factors*.mat'))`. Also update the stale docstring at line 96 to reflect the current `Rep_ANOVA_Main_effect_*` / `Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_Factors_*` naming.
verifier_reasoning: Verified at limo_random_robust.m:1052. The guard `if isempty(dir('Rep_ANOVA_Factor*.mat'))` wraps the entire rep-measures computation. The files this branch writes are named `Rep_ANOVA_Main_effect_%g...` (1158/1160), `Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_Factors_%s...` (1165), `Rep_ANOVA_Interaction_gp_Factor(s)_*` (1184/1186/1191), and `Rep_ANOVA_Gp_effect.mat` (1206). None begins with the literal `Rep_ANOVA_Factor`, so the wildcard `Rep_ANOVA_Factor*.mat` never matches. A repo-wide grep confirms `Rep_ANOVA_Factor` appears only in an outdated docstring at line 96 (`Rep_ANOVA_Factor_X`, an old naming scheme) and in this guard — no code path ever creates such a file. Therefore `dir(...)` is always empty, the guard is always true, and the intended "skip if already computed" behavior is permanently disabled: every invocation recomputes and overwrites prior results. The presence of the fallback at line 1214 (`if ~exist('nb_effects','var'), nb_effects = length(LIMO.design.C); end`) with the comment "if skipping the above" is direct evidence the skip path was intended to be reachable. No crash occurs because the block always executes (so Rep_filenames/IRep_filenames are always defined before later loops). This is a real implementation defect but purely behavioral: it causes redundant recomputation and silent overwrite of existing outputs, not incorrect statistics or a crash — hence low severity. Not a duplicate: no listed finding touches line 1052 or the skip-guard filename mismatch (known findings are at 708, 1342, 157, 351, 477, 604, 687, 1442).

summary: Between-group indicator matrix Gp assigns rows non-cumulatively, so for designs with 3 or more groups the group membership matrix is corrupted.
detail: In both case 3 (lines 407-415) and case 4 (lines 589-597) the loop building the group design matrix does `index2 = nb_items(index1) + 1;` after filling `Gp(index2:index2+nb_items(index1)-1,i)=1`. `index2` is reset to `nb_items(current_group)+1` rather than advanced cumulatively (should be `index2 = index2 + nb_items(index1)`). For group 1 and group 2 the offsets happen to be correct, but from group 3 onward the starting row is wrong: group 3's indicator is written into the same rows previously used by group 2, and the true group-3 subject rows are never marked. The corrupted Gp then propagates into X, the SS_Gp / SS_XGp / SS error terms, and every F/p in results. This only manifests with 3+ between-subject groups (2-group designs are unaffected because index2 for group 2 = nb_items(1)+1 is coincidentally correct).
failure_scenario: Call limo_rep_anova_old directly with a Gp*factor repeated-measures design (case 3) or multi-factor version (case 4) with 3 groups of n subjects each, gp = [ones(n,1);2*ones(n,1);3*ones(n,1)]. The loop places group 3's indicator at rows n+1..2n (already group 2's rows) and leaves rows 2n+1..3n unmarked. The resulting Gp/X are corrupted, and the group main-effect and group-by-factor interaction F/p values are computed on a wrong design (silently wrong, not a crash).
minimal_fix: In both loops replace `index2 = nb_items(index1) + 1;` (line ~413 and line ~595) with `index2 = index2 + nb_items(index1);` so the row pointer advances cumulatively across groups.
verifier_reasoning: Real bug. In case 3 (408-415) and case 4 (589-597) the group-indicator loop sets `index2 = nb_items(index1) + 1;` which resets the row pointer to the size of the just-processed group rather than advancing it cumulatively. Group 1 start (=1) and group 2 start (=nb_items(1)+1) are correct only coincidentally; from group 3 onward the start row is nb_items(prev)+1 instead of the cumulative sum, so group 3's column is written into group 2's rows and group 3's true rows are left all-zero. Gp then feeds X=[Gp x XGp S] and all SS_Gp/SS_XGp/error terms, so every between-group F/p is wrong for designs with 3+ groups. NOT a duplicate of the known line-481 finding, which concerns the interaction p-value using the factor's numerator df — a different root cause. Severity lowered to low because limo_rep_anova_old has no callers in the toolbox (grep shows only self-references); it is a legacy file, so impact is limited to direct manual invocation. When reached, impact is a silently corrupted design and incorrect statistics.

summary: Time-Frequency titles use %g with a char effect number, printing ASCII codes instead of the effect index
detail: In the Time-Frequency branch, effect_nb comes from extractAfter(FileName,...) and is a char (e.g. '1'). Lines 169 (`Semi Partial Coef %g`), 179 (`Contrast %g T values`) and 197 (`Contrast %g F values`) pass this char to a %g conversion. sprintf('%g','1') prints the numeric char code (49), not '1', and for multi-digit ids (char '10' = [49 48]) sprintf recycles the format producing 'Contrast 49 ... Contrast 48 ...'. The non-Time-Frequency branch correctly uses %s at the analogous lines 249/259/277, confirming %g is a mistake.
failure_scenario: Display a Time-Frequency first-level contrast file con_1_....mat: the figure title renders 'Contrast 49 T values' instead of 'Contrast 1 T values'. A con_10 file (effect_nb='10'=[49 48]) yields a doubled/garbled 'Contrast 4948 T values'. Likewise semi_partial_coef_1 -> 'Semi Partial Coef 49' (line 169) and ess_1 -> 'Contrast 49 F values' (line 197). Statistics and thresholding are correct; only the title string is wrong.
minimal_fix: Change %g to %s at lines 169, 179, and 197 to match the non-Time-Frequency branch (or wrap effect_nb with str2double before using %g).
verifier_reasoning: In the Time-Frequency branch, effect_nb = extractAfter(FileName,'con_') returns a char array (FileName is a char filename). Line 179 passes this char to sprintf('%g',...). MATLAB's %g conversion coerces a char to its numeric code(s): sprintf('%g','1') -> '49' (ASCII of '1'), and for '10' = [49 48] the format recycles producing '4948'. The same defect exists at line 169 ('Semi Partial Coef %g') and 197 ('Contrast %g F values'). The parallel non-Time-Frequency branch (lines 249/259/277) correctly uses %s, confirming %g is a copy error, not intentional. Impact is confined to on-screen figure titles; the statistical maps (M, Pval, mask, MCC_data filenames) are unaffected since those use %s correctly. Hence a real but purely cosmetic defect. Not a duplicate: the known findings on this file concern the R2 dispatch (201), H0 infix filename (135), and single-channel TF bootstrap-dimension bugs (456/503/510) — different root causes and lines.

summary: t-test title name extraction uses lowercase 'ttest' in strfind, which never matches capital 'Ttest' filenames, yielding an empty title prefix
detail: Lines 185 and 265 build the display name with `name = FileName(1:strfind(FileName,'ttest')+4)`. Real t-test filenames contain capital 'Ttest' (limo_random_robust.m), so the case-sensitive strfind returns [], making `1:([]+4)` an empty range and `name` an empty char. The resulting title is just ' t-test T values' with the test description dropped (and LI_Map files, which contain no 'ttest' at all, are likewise left blank).
failure_scenario: Call limo_stat_values with FileName='One_Sample_Ttest_parameter_1.mat' (LIMO.Level==2, uncorrected MCC==1). After fileparts, FileName='One_Sample_Ttest_parameter_1'. The branch at :180/:260 is entered because contains(...,'Ttest') is true. At :185/:265 strfind(FileName,'ttest') returns [] (case mismatch: file has capital 'Ttest'), so `1:[]+4` is empty, name='', and the figure title renders as ' T values' (2D case) / ' t-test T values' (3D case) instead of 'One Sample Ttest ... T values'. LI_Map files hit the same branch and likewise get an empty name.
minimal_fix: Locate the token case-insensitively before extracting, e.g. `name = FileName(1:strfind(lower(FileName),'ttest')+4);` at both lines 185 and 265. For full correctness of the LI_Map path (no 'ttest' token), fall back to using the whole FileName when the token is absent (e.g. `idx = strfind(lower(FileName),'ttest'); if isempty(idx), name = FileName; else, name = FileName(1:idx+4); end`), then replace underscores with spaces as before.
verifier_reasoning: Real but purely cosmetic (figure-title only) defect at limo_stat_values.m:185 and its 2D twin at :265. Both lines run only inside the branch guarded by `contains(FileName,'Ttest') || contains(FileName,'LI_Map')`, which is case-sensitive and matches capital `Ttest`. FileName is derived via `fileparts` (line 65), preserving original case, and limo_random_robust.m always writes capital-T names (`One_Sample_Ttest_parameter_%g`, `Two_Samples_Ttest_parameter_...`, `Paired_Samples_Ttest_parameter_...`). Thus `strfind(FileName,'ttest')` (lowercase) always returns []. In MATLAB `[]+4` is [], `1:[]` is a 1x0 empty, `FileName(1:[])` is '', and the subsequent `name(strfind(name,'_'))=' '` is a no-op on empty. sprintf then yields ' t-test T values' (line 187) / ' T values' (line 267) with the descriptive prefix dropped. No crash, no wrong statistics — M, Pval, mask, thresholds are all unaffected; only the displayed/saved title string is wrong. Severity low. Not a duplicate: the known findings cover R2.mat dispatch (:201), TF H0 filename infix (:135), and single-channel TF bootstrap-dim sizing (:456/:503/:510) — different lines and root causes; none address the Ttest title casing at :185/:265. Note the LI_Map arm of the same branch is also left with an empty name (LI_Map filenames contain no 'ttest' token at all), a related sub-issue the reported fix only partially addresses.

summary: Variable 'parameter' is used in filename and fprintf but never defined anywhere in the function
detail: `parameter` is referenced on line 65 (`sprintf('H0_one_sample_ttest_parameter_%g',parameter)`), line 89, and line 136, but it is never assigned nor passed in as an argument. Even if the `varagin` typos were fixed, the type-1 path would crash at line 65 building `boot_name`, and the type-0 limo_trimci path would crash at line 136.
failure_scenario: Under current code, limo_compute_H0(1,data,label,nboot) does NOT reach line 65: it crashes earlier at line 38 (`if type == 0` where type is a 1x1 cell -> 'Operator == not supported for cell'). Only if line 38 and the `varagin` typos on lines 45/49 were fixed would the type-1 path reach line 65 and then crash at `sprintf('...parameter_%g',parameter)` with 'Undefined function or variable parameter' (and likewise line 136 in the type-0 limo_trimci path). The undefined `parameter` is therefore a real but currently-masked/unreachable defect in a function that has no callers anywhere in the toolbox.
minimal_fix: Add `parameter` as an input argument and assign it before use (e.g. extract from varargin), or remove `parameter` from the filename/fprintf strings on lines 65, 89, and 136. Note this bug is moot until the upstream line-35 cell-indexing (`varargin{1}`) and `varagin`->`varargin` typos are also fixed.
verifier_reasoning: `parameter` is genuinely undefined: grep confirms it appears only as a read at lines 65, 89, 136, with no assignment and no varargin extraction, so any execution of those lines would throw 'Undefined function or variable parameter'. This is a real latent defect. However, it is NOT independently reachable: line 35 does `type = varargin(1)` (cell), so line 38 `if type == 0` compares a cell to a double and errors first on every input (the already-known finding). Even fixing that, the data-extraction lines use the misspelled `varagin(2)/(3)` (an undefined function) and crash at lines 45/49/52 before line 65. So no concrete input reaches the `parameter` reference — the crash that actually occurs is the upstream one. Hence PLAUSIBLE, not CONFIRMED. It is a distinct root cause (undefined local var at 65/89/136) from the known line-38 cell-comparison finding, so not a duplicate. Severity low: the whole function is dead code (grep -rln shows zero callers outside the file itself) and is already non-functional from the upstream crashes, so the undefined-parameter bug has no practical impact.
